fields.title: Optics
fields.description: Master the principles of geometrical and visual optics, refraction, lenses, and image formation relevant to ophthalmic instruments and refractive errors.

fields.title: Instruments and Investigations
fields.description: Learn how ophthalmic instruments function, from slit lamps to OCT and fundus imaging, and interpret key diagnostic findings.

fields.title: Epidemiology and Biostatistics
fields.description: Understand study design, data interpretation, and statistical methods applied in ophthalmic research and clinical trials.

fields.title: Genetics
fields.description: Examine the genetic basis of ocular diseases, inheritance patterns, and recent advances in molecular ophthalmology.

fields.title: Anatomy
fields.description: Review ocular and orbital anatomy, extraocular muscles, cranial nerves, and key structural relationships essential for ophthalmic surgery and examination.

fields.title: Physiology
fields.description: Study the physiology of vision, accommodation, intraocular pressure regulation, and tear film dynamics.

fields.title: Cell Biology
fields.description: Learn the fundamentals of cell structure, signaling, organelles, and molecular mechanisms of life.

fields.title: Pathology
fields.description: Explore the microscopic and macroscopic pathology of ocular tissues, including inflammatory, degenerative, and neoplastic eye diseases

fields.title: Immunology
fields.description: Focus on ocular immune privilege, hypersensitivity reactions, and autoimmune mechanisms involved in eye disease.

fields.title: Microbiology
fields.description: Study the pathogens responsible for ocular infections and understand sterilisation and antimicrobial principles in ophthalmology.

fields.title: Pharmacology
fields.description: Learn the pharmacodynamics and pharmacokinetics of ophthalmic drugs, including mydriatics, miotics, and antiglaucoma agents.

fields.text: Which statement regarding contact lens correction is FALSE:
fields.option_a: contact lens power is calculated from the spectacle correction and the back vertex distance
fields.option_b: truncation of lenses provides greater stability
fields.option_c: soft contact lens correction of astigmatism requires a toric lens
fields.option_d: keratoconus can be corrected by a hard contact lens
fields.option_e: in myopes, contact lenses reduce the need for convergence compared with spectacles
fields.explanation: Contact lens correction of myopia increases the need for convergence because of the loss of the base-in effect of the spectacle lenses. All other statements are correct.

fields.text: What is the ratio of cones to ganglion cells in the fovea?
fields.explanation: In the fovea, each cone synapses with a single bipolar and ganglion cell, resulting in a 1:1 ratio which provides high visual acuity.

fields.text: Which statement regarding the fovea is TRUE:
fields.option_a: It contains more rods than cones
fields.option_b: It is the area of lowest visual acuity
fields.option_c: It lacks blood vessels in the foveal avascular zone
fields.option_d: It receives input mainly via the magnocellular pathway
fields.option_e: It is responsible for peripheral vision
fields.explanation: The fovea contains only cones and lacks retinal blood vessels in the foveal avascular zone to allow unobstructed passage of light for maximum acuity.

fields.text: Which statement regarding rods and cones is FALSE:
fields.option_a: Rods are more sensitive to dim light
fields.option_c: Rods are concentrated in the fovea
fields.option_e: Rod signals are transmitted mainly through the magnocellular pathway
fields.explanation: Rods are absent in the fovea and are most numerous in the peripheral retina where they mediate scotopic (low light) vision.

fields.text: In the peripheral retina, the approximate ratio of rods to ganglion cells is:
fields.explanation: In the peripheral retina, many rods converge on single ganglion cells resulting in a ratio of roughly 10,000:1 which increases light sensitivity at the expense of resolution.

fields.text: Which retinal layer contains the synapses between photoreceptors and bipolar cells:
fields.explanation: The outer plexiform layer is where photoreceptors synapse with bipolar and horizontal cells, forming the first step of visual signal processing.

fields.text: Which of the following statements about the parvocellular pathway is TRUE:
fields.option_a: It carries information about motion and flicker
fields.option_c: It transmits fine detail and colour information
fields.option_d: It projects to laminae 1 and 2 of the lateral geniculate body
fields.option_e: It is faster than the magnocellular pathway
fields.explanation: The parvocellular pathway originates from midget ganglion cells and carries colour and high spatial resolution information to laminae 3–6 of the LGN.

fields.text: The magnocellular pathway is primarily involved in:
fields.explanation: The magnocellular pathway originates from parasol ganglion cells and is specialised for motion detection and high temporal resolution.

fields.text: Which of the following best describes the distribution of photoreceptors in the retina:
fields.option_a: Rods are most dense in the fovea
fields.option_b: Cones are uniformly distributed across the retina
fields.option_c: Rods peak in density at about 20 degrees eccentricity from the fovea
fields.option_d: The optic disc has a high concentration of rods
fields.option_e: Cones outnumber rods in the peripheral retina
fields.explanation: Rod density peaks around 20 degrees from the fovea and declines towards both the foveal and peripheral extremes.

fields.text: Which statement regarding retinal ganglion cells is FALSE:
fields.option_a: Parvocellular ganglion cells have small receptive fields
fields.option_b: Magnocellular ganglion cells are sensitive to motion
fields.option_c: Parvocellular cells respond well to colour contrast
fields.explanation: Magnocellular cells are achromatic and process luminance and motion information, while parvocellular cells handle colour and fine spatial detail.

fields.text: Which structure corresponds to the first site of visual signal convergence in the retina:
fields.explanation: The outer plexiform layer is the first site of convergence where multiple photoreceptors synapse on shared bipolar and horizontal cells.

fields.text: Midget ganglion cells and midget bipolar cells are most common in:
fields.explanation: Midget ganglion and bipolar cells dominate in the fovea where they connect in a 1:1:1 ratio with cones, contributing to the high spatial resolution of central vision.

fields.text: Which statement about midget ganglion cells is TRUE:
fields.option_a: They are primarily involved in motion detection
fields.option_c: They transmit signals via the magnocellular pathway
fields.option_d: They have small receptive fields and high spatial resolution
fields.option_e: They receive input mainly from rods
fields.explanation: Midget ganglion cells receive input from single midget bipolar cells and cones, giving them small receptive fields and excellent spatial and colour resolution.

fields.text: Which statement about parasol ganglion cells is FALSE:
fields.option_a: They project to the magnocellular layers of the LGN
fields.option_b: They respond best to high temporal frequencies
fields.option_e: They are involved in motion detection
fields.explanation: Parasol ganglion cells are achromatic and transmit luminance and motion information rather than colour, unlike midget cells which process red-green colour signals.

fields.text: Which of the following is characteristic of the foveal region:
fields.option_b: It has multiple layers of ganglion cells
fields.option_c: It contains the highest concentration of cones
fields.option_d: It receives most input from parasol cells
fields.explanation: The fovea contains only cones that connect one-to-one with bipolar and ganglion cells, providing maximal resolution and colour discrimination.

fields.text: Which statement best describes receptive fields in the fovea:
fields.option_a: They are large and suited for motion detection
fields.option_b: They are small and suited for fine spatial resolution
fields.option_d: They are absent due to the foveal pit
fields.option_e: They are influenced mainly by rod input
fields.explanation: Foveal receptive fields are small due to the one-to-one relationship between cones and ganglion cells, allowing high spatial detail detection.

fields.text: Which of the following statements regarding the magnocellular and parvocellular systems is TRUE:
fields.option_a: Parvocellular neurons have faster conduction than magnocellular neurons
fields.option_b: Magnocellular neurons are sensitive to colour contrast
fields.option_c: Magnocellular neurons project to laminae 1 and 2 of the LGN
fields.option_d: Parvocellular neurons project to laminae 1 and 2 of the LGN
fields.option_e: Both systems transmit fine colour discrimination equally
fields.explanation: Magnocellular neurons project to laminae 1 and 2 of the LGN and mediate motion detection, while parvocellular neurons project to laminae 3–6 and handle colour and form.

fields.text: Which feature is most characteristic of midget bipolar cells:
fields.option_b: They are responsible for scotopic vision
fields.option_c: They connect with a single cone and a single ganglion cell
fields.option_d: They are found primarily in the retinal periphery
fields.option_e: They project to the magnocellular pathway
fields.explanation: Midget bipolar cells connect one-to-one with cones and midget ganglion cells in the fovea, forming the core of the parvocellular visual pathway.

fields.text: Which of the following best explains the high resolution of the foveal region:
fields.option_b: High degree of convergence of photoreceptors
fields.option_c: One-to-one connections between cones and ganglion cells
fields.option_d: Presence of multiple ganglion cell layers
fields.option_e: Greater blood supply from the choriocapillaris
fields.explanation: Each cone in the fovea connects through its own bipolar and ganglion cell, preventing signal convergence and ensuring fine spatial resolution.

fields.text: Which type of ganglion cell contributes to the koniocellular pathway:
fields.explanation: Small bistratified ganglion cells transmit blue-yellow colour information via the koniocellular pathway, distinct from the magnocellular and parvocellular systems.

fields.text: Which statement about retinal signal transmission is FALSE:
fields.option_a: Midget cells dominate in the fovea
fields.option_b: Parasol cells dominate in the periphery
fields.option_c: Each cone in the fovea connects to multiple ganglion cells
fields.option_d: Horizontal cells mediate lateral inhibition in the retina
fields.option_e: Amacrine cells modulate ganglion cell responses
fields.explanation: In the fovea, each cone synapses with a single bipolar and ganglion cell. Multiple convergence occurs mainly in the periphery.

fields.text: Which statement is FALSE about the corneal stroma:
fields.option_a: the collagenous lamellae are oriented parallel to the corneal surface
fields.option_b: collagenous lamellae are orientated at right angles to each other
fields.option_c: it is composed of 20–40 layers of flattened collagenous lamellae
fields.option_d: it is a dense connective tissue of high regularity
fields.option_e: it accounts for approximately 90% of corneal thickness
fields.explanation: The corneal stroma is composed of approximately 200–250 layers of flattened collagenous lamellae arranged parallel to the surface. Other statements are true.

fields.text: Which type of collagen is most abundant in the corneal stroma:
fields.explanation: The corneal stroma is primarily composed of type I collagen with smaller amounts of type V collagen, which helps regulate fibril diameter and maintain transparency.

fields.text: What proportion of the total corneal thickness does the stroma account for:
fields.explanation: The stroma makes up around 90% of the total corneal thickness, providing structural strength and maintaining the cornea’s transparency.

fields.text: Which statement about corneal transparency is TRUE:
fields.option_a: Transparency depends on the random orientation of collagen fibrils
fields.option_b: Transparency is maintained by destructive interference between scattered light waves
fields.option_c: Transparency is due to pigmentation of the stroma
fields.option_e: Transparency is dependent on Bowman's layer thickness
fields.explanation: Corneal transparency is maintained by the uniform spacing and diameter of collagen fibrils causing destructive interference of scattered light, reducing haze.

fields.text: Which cell type is responsible for synthesising stromal collagen and proteoglycans:
fields.explanation: Keratocytes, located between stromal lamellae, are responsible for producing collagen and extracellular matrix components essential for corneal transparency and repair.

fields.text: Which proteoglycan contributes most to the regular spacing of collagen fibrils in the stroma:
fields.explanation: Lumican and keratocan are keratan sulfate proteoglycans that play a major role in maintaining uniform collagen fibril spacing and thus corneal transparency.

fields.text: Which statement best describes the orientation of collagen fibrils in the corneal stroma:
fields.option_a: They are arranged irregularly to maximise strength
fields.option_b: They are arranged radially from the limbus
fields.option_c: They run parallel to the corneal surface and at right angles in adjacent lamellae
fields.option_d: They spiral toward the corneal apex
fields.option_e: They intersect at random angles for light diffraction
fields.explanation: Collagen fibrils in the corneal stroma run parallel to the surface and at right angles in adjacent lamellae, creating a highly ordered lattice crucial for transparency.

fields.text: Which of the following statements about the posterior corneal stroma is TRUE:
fields.option_a: It contains fewer keratocytes than the anterior stroma
fields.option_b: It has thicker collagen fibrils than the anterior stroma
fields.option_c: It contributes to more light scattering than the anterior stroma
fields.option_d: It is continuous with Descemet’s membrane
fields.option_e: It contains Bowman’s layer beneath it
fields.explanation: The posterior stroma contains fewer keratocytes and less interweaving of collagen fibrils compared to the anterior stroma, which contributes to its greater strength.

fields.text: Which of the following statements about the corneal lamellae is FALSE:
fields.option_a: They are evenly spaced throughout the stroma
fields.option_b: They interweave more in the anterior third of the stroma
fields.option_d: They contribute to the cornea’s tensile strength
fields.option_e: They are arranged randomly in all directions
fields.explanation: Corneal lamellae are highly ordered and parallel, particularly in the posterior stroma. Only the anterior third shows some interweaving; random arrangement would cause opacity.

fields.text: Which layer of the cornea lies directly anterior to the stroma:
fields.explanation: Bowman’s layer lies anterior to the stroma and consists of randomly arranged collagen fibrils providing structural support but limited regenerative capacity.

fields.text: Which statement most accurately describes the thickness of the human cornea?
fields.option_a: 0.5 – 0.6 mm in the centre and 0.6 – 0.8 mm at the periphery
fields.option_b: 0.3 – 0.5 mm in the centre and 0.4 – 0.6 mm at the periphery
fields.option_c: 0.3 – 0.6 mm in the centre and 0.7 – 0.9 mm at the periphery
fields.option_d: 0.5 – 0.6 mm in the centre and 0.5 – 0.7 mm at the periphery
fields.option_e: 0.4 – 0.5 mm in the centre and 0.5 – 0.6 mm at the periphery
fields.explanation: The human cornea is thinnest centrally at around 0.5–0.6 mm and thickens towards the periphery to about 0.7–0.8 mm.

fields.text: Which of the following layers contributes most to corneal thickness?
fields.explanation: The stroma makes up approximately 90% of the corneal thickness and is composed of regularly arranged collagen lamellae.

fields.text: Which of the following statements about corneal hydration is TRUE?
fields.option_a: Normal corneal water content is approximately 80%
fields.option_b: Corneal swelling results in decreased transparency
fields.option_c: The endothelium contributes minimally to deturgescence
fields.option_e: Increased tear osmolarity increases corneal thickness
fields.explanation: Excess water uptake disrupts the regular collagen spacing, increasing light scatter and reducing corneal transparency.

fields.text: What is the approximate percentage of water in the normal corneal stroma?
fields.explanation: The corneal stroma is approximately 78% water by weight, a balance maintained by endothelial pump function and tear film osmolarity.

fields.text: Which mechanism primarily maintains corneal deturgescence?
fields.explanation: The corneal endothelium maintains deturgescence via active ion transport through Na+/K+ ATPase and bicarbonate pumps, drawing fluid out of the stroma.

fields.text: Which statement about the corneal periphery compared to the centre is TRUE?
fields.option_a: The periphery is thinner and flatter
fields.option_b: The periphery is thicker and steeper
fields.option_c: The periphery is thicker and flatter
fields.option_d: The periphery is thinner and steeper
fields.option_e: Thickness and curvature remain constant across the cornea
fields.explanation: The corneal periphery is thicker and flatter than the central cornea, helping maintain the overall refractive shape of the cornea.

fields.text: Which factor contributes most to the cornea’s refractive power?
fields.explanation: The anterior corneal surface contributes approximately two-thirds of the total refractive power of the eye, primarily due to the air-tear interface.

fields.text: Which measurement technique most accurately assesses central corneal thickness?
fields.explanation: Ultrasound pachymetry provides accurate and reproducible measurements of central corneal thickness and is the gold standard in clinical use.

fields.text: Which statement about corneal swelling is FALSE?
fields.option_a: Swelling primarily occurs in the posterior stroma
fields.option_b: Corneal thickness may double after endothelial failure
fields.option_c: The corneal epithelium swells more than the stroma
fields.option_d: Hypoxia can lead to corneal oedema
fields.option_e: Loss of endothelial function increases stromal hydration
fields.explanation: The corneal stroma, not the epithelium, swells most during oedema due to its hydrophilic proteoglycans. Epithelial swelling is limited by tight junctions.

fields.text: Which statement about the cornea’s biomechanical properties is TRUE?
fields.option_a: The cornea has uniform stiffness across its surface
fields.option_b: The cornea is more rigid centrally than peripherally
fields.option_c: The cornea is more elastic peripherally than centrally
fields.option_d: The cornea resists deformation equally in all directions
fields.explanation: The cornea is more elastic at the periphery due to increased collagen lamellae interweaving, while the central cornea is thinner and less deformable.

fields.text: The ophthalmic artery runs:
fields.option_e: directly through the optic nerve sheath
fields.explanation: The ophthalmic artery runs inferolateral to the optic nerve after entering the orbit through the optic canal and then crosses superior to the nerve.

fields.text: The ophthalmic artery is a branch of which vessel:
fields.explanation: The ophthalmic artery arises from the internal carotid artery just after it emerges from the cavernous sinus, entering the orbit via the optic canal.

fields.text: After entering the orbit, the ophthalmic artery first gives rise to which branch:
fields.explanation: The first major branch of the ophthalmic artery is usually the central retinal artery, which enters the optic nerve approximately 1 cm posterior to the globe.

fields.text: Which statement about the ophthalmic artery is TRUE:
fields.option_a: It enters the orbit via the superior orbital fissure
fields.option_b: It runs lateral to the optic nerve throughout its course
fields.option_c: It passes inferior to the optic nerve and then crosses superiorly
fields.option_d: It runs entirely within the annulus of Zinn
fields.explanation: The ophthalmic artery enters the orbit inferolateral to the optic nerve and then crosses superior to it, continuing medially toward the medial orbital wall.

fields.text: Which branch of the ophthalmic artery supplies the lacrimal gland:
fields.explanation: The lacrimal artery, a lateral branch of the ophthalmic artery, supplies the lacrimal gland, eyelids, and portions of the conjunctiva.

fields.text: Which of the following arteries is a terminal branch of the ophthalmic artery:
fields.explanation: The ophthalmic artery terminates by dividing into the supratrochlear and dorsal nasal arteries near the medial canthus.

fields.text: The central retinal artery typically enters the optic nerve:
fields.explanation: The central retinal artery enters the optic nerve approximately 10–12 mm behind the globe and supplies the inner retinal layers.

fields.text: Which structure does the posterior ciliary arteries supply:
fields.explanation: The posterior ciliary arteries branch from the ophthalmic artery and supply the choroid, ciliary body, and optic nerve head.

fields.text: The dorsal nasal artery is a terminal branch of which vessel:
fields.explanation: The dorsal nasal artery is one of the terminal branches of the ophthalmic artery and anastomoses with the angular branch of the facial artery.

fields.text: Which clinical condition may result from occlusion of the ophthalmic artery:
fields.option_d: Ophthalmic artery occlusion causing sudden total blindness
fields.explanation: Ophthalmic artery occlusion causes sudden, profound monocular blindness due to interruption of blood flow to both the retinal and choroidal circulations.

fields.text: The lacrimal sac fossa is made up of:
fields.explanation: The lacrimal fossa is formed by the frontal process of the maxillary bone and the lacrimal bone. It houses the lacrimal sac within the medial orbital wall.

fields.text: The nasolacrimal duct opens into which part of the nasal cavity:
fields.explanation: The nasolacrimal duct drains tears from the lacrimal sac into the inferior meatus of the nasal cavity, below the inferior turbinate.

fields.text: The lacrimal gland receives its parasympathetic secretomotor supply from:
fields.option_b: facial nerve via greater petrosal nerve
fields.option_e: nasociliary branch of the ophthalmic nerve
fields.explanation: Parasympathetic fibres to the lacrimal gland originate in the facial nerve, travel via the greater petrosal and zygomatic branches, and synapse in the pterygopalatine ganglion.

fields.text: Which statement about the lacrimal sac is TRUE:
fields.option_a: It lies posterior to the posterior lacrimal crest
fields.option_b: It lies in the fossa formed by the zygomatic and lacrimal bones
fields.option_c: It lies anterior to the medial palpebral ligament
fields.option_d: It lies within the lacrimal fossa formed by the maxillary and lacrimal bones
fields.option_e: It lies lateral to the orbital septum
fields.explanation: The lacrimal sac lies within the lacrimal fossa between the anterior and posterior lacrimal crests, formed by the frontal process of the maxilla and the lacrimal bone.

fields.text: Which structure passes between the lacrimal sac and the orbital margin:
fields.explanation: The medial palpebral ligament passes anterior to the lacrimal sac, anchoring the tarsal plates to the medial orbital wall.

fields.text: Which bone forms the posterior lacrimal crest:
fields.explanation: The posterior lacrimal crest is formed by the lacrimal bone, while the anterior lacrimal crest is part of the maxillary bone.

fields.text: The lacrimal gland is divided into orbital and palpebral lobes by which structure:
fields.explanation: The levator palpebrae superioris aponeurosis divides the lacrimal gland into a larger orbital lobe and a smaller palpebral lobe.

fields.text: Which statement about the nasolacrimal duct is FALSE:
fields.option_b: It opens into the inferior meatus of the nose
fields.option_d: It runs vertically upwards from the lacrimal sac
fields.option_e: It is lined by pseudostratified columnar epithelium
fields.explanation: The nasolacrimal duct runs downward, backward, and slightly laterally from the lacrimal sac into the inferior nasal meatus. It is about 18 mm long and lined with respiratory epithelium.

fields.text: Which muscle aids in the drainage of tears by compressing the lacrimal sac:
fields.explanation: Contraction of the lacrimal part of the orbicularis oculi (Horner’s muscle) compresses the lacrimal sac, aiding tear drainage into the nasolacrimal duct.

fields.text: The lacrimal gland lies within which structure:
fields.option_b: lacrimal fossa of the frontal bone
fields.explanation: The lacrimal gland occupies the lacrimal fossa in the superolateral aspect of the orbit, formed by the frontal bone.

fields.text: Which statement is FALSE about the conjunctiva:
fields.option_a: its epithelium is continuous with the lid skin at the eye lid margin
fields.option_b: it is bound loosely to the tarsal plate
fields.option_c: it is responsible for the production of the mucous component of the tear film
fields.option_d: its epithelium is continuous with the corneal epithelium at the limbus
fields.option_e: goblet cells are found within its epithelium
fields.explanation: The palpebral conjunctiva is firmly adherent to the tarsal plate, not loosely bound. It is continuous with the corneal epithelium at the limbus and with the skin at the lid margin. Goblet cells within it produce the mucous layer of the tear film.

fields.text: Goblet cells within the conjunctiva are most densely concentrated in which region:
fields.explanation: Goblet cells are most numerous in the conjunctival fornices and the inferonasal bulbar conjunctiva, where they secrete mucins that form part of the tear film.

fields.text: Which statement about the bulbar conjunctiva is TRUE:
fields.option_a: It is loosely attached to the underlying sclera at the limbus
fields.option_b: It is continuous with the corneal epithelium at the limbus
fields.option_c: It is firmly adherent to Tenon’s capsule
fields.option_d: It is highly keratinised in healthy individuals
fields.option_e: It contains the main lacrimal gland ducts
fields.explanation: The bulbar conjunctiva is continuous with the corneal epithelium at the limbus and is normally non-keratinised. It is loosely attached to Tenon’s capsule except at the limbus where adhesion is firm.

fields.text: The epithelial lining of the conjunctiva is best described as:
fields.option_c: non-keratinised stratified columnar epithelium with goblet cells
fields.explanation: The conjunctival epithelium is a non-keratinised stratified columnar type containing goblet cells that secrete mucins essential for tear film stability.

fields.text: Which artery primarily supplies the palpebral conjunctiva:
fields.explanation: The palpebral conjunctiva receives its blood supply from the medial and lateral palpebral arteries, both branches of the ophthalmic artery.

fields.text: Which structure separates the conjunctival epithelium from the sclera:
fields.explanation: Tenon’s capsule (the fascial sheath of the eyeball) lies between the conjunctiva and sclera and separates them anatomically.

fields.text: The mucous layer of the tear film is secreted primarily by:
fields.explanation: Goblet cells within the conjunctiva secrete mucins that form the innermost layer of the tear film, aiding wetting of the corneal epithelium.

fields.text: Which part of the conjunctiva is most firmly adherent to underlying tissue:
fields.explanation: The palpebral conjunctiva is tightly adherent to the underlying tarsal plate, unlike the bulbar conjunctiva which is more mobile.

fields.text: Which nerve provides sensory innervation to the bulbar conjunctiva:
fields.explanation: The nasociliary branch of the ophthalmic division of the trigeminal nerve (CN V1) provides sensory innervation to the bulbar conjunctiva.

fields.text: Which layer of the tear film directly contacts the conjunctival epithelium:
fields.explanation: The mucous layer, produced by conjunctival goblet cells, lies directly against the conjunctival and corneal epithelia, allowing even distribution of the aqueous tear layer.

fields.text: Which layer overlies the Bowman's layer:
fields.explanation: Overlying Bowman’s layer is the basal lamina of the corneal epithelium, which has two layers: the superficial lamina lucida and the deeper lamina densa.

fields.text: Which of the following statements about Bowman’s layer is TRUE:
fields.option_b: It is secreted by the corneal endothelium
fields.option_c: It is an acellular condensation of the anterior stroma
fields.option_d: It forms the basement membrane of the corneal epithelium
fields.explanation: Bowman’s layer is an acellular, collagen-rich condensation of the anterior corneal stroma. It does not regenerate after injury.

fields.text: Which statement best describes Descemet’s membrane:
fields.option_a: It is the basement membrane of the corneal epithelium
fields.option_b: It is acellular and secreted by the corneal endothelium
fields.option_c: It contains Bowman’s fibres and keratocytes
fields.option_e: It lies superficial to Bowman’s layer
fields.explanation: Descemet’s membrane is a strong, acellular layer secreted by the corneal endothelium. It serves as its basement membrane and regenerates after injury.

fields.text: Which corneal layer is most responsible for maintaining corneal dehydration and transparency:
fields.explanation: The corneal endothelium actively pumps ions out of the stroma, preventing excessive hydration. Its integrity is essential for corneal transparency.

fields.text: Which of the following is the thickest layer of the human cornea:
fields.explanation: The corneal stroma comprises approximately 90% of corneal thickness and consists of regularly arranged collagen lamellae with keratocytes.

fields.text: Which statement about the corneal epithelium is TRUE:
fields.option_a: It is a keratinised stratified squamous epithelium
fields.option_b: It is non-keratinised and regenerates rapidly
fields.option_c: It has a turnover time of approximately 30 days
fields.option_d: It lacks tight junctions between cells
fields.option_e: It is thicker peripherally than centrally
fields.explanation: The corneal epithelium is a non-keratinised stratified squamous epithelium with tight junctions in superficial cells. It regenerates rapidly, typically within 7 days.

fields.text: The interface between Descemet’s membrane and the stroma is known as:
fields.explanation: Dua’s layer, a strong acellular zone approximately 10 microns thick, lies between the posterior stroma and Descemet’s membrane.

fields.text: Which statement regarding corneal nerves is FALSE:
fields.option_a: They are derived from the nasociliary nerve
fields.option_b: They lose their myelin sheaths before entering the stroma
fields.option_c: They enter the cornea circumferentially at the limbus
fields.option_d: They traverse Descemet’s membrane to reach the endothelium
fields.option_e: They form subepithelial and intraepithelial plexuses
fields.explanation: Corneal nerves do not penetrate Descemet’s membrane or reach the endothelium. They enter the stroma at the limbus and form plexuses within anterior layers.

fields.text: Which structure forms the anterior limiting membrane of the cornea:
fields.explanation: Bowman’s layer is also known as the anterior limiting membrane. It lies beneath the basal lamina of the corneal epithelium and provides structural rigidity.

fields.text: Which of the following corneal layers can regenerate after injury:
fields.explanation: The corneal epithelium regenerates rapidly, and Descemet’s membrane can regenerate. Bowman’s layer does not regenerate after damage.

fields.text: From anterior to posterior the medial orbital wall is composed of:
fields.explanation: The medial orbital wall is composed (anterior to posterior) of: the frontal process of the maxilla, the lacrimal bone, the orbital plate of the ethmoid, and the body of the sphenoid.

fields.text: Which bone contributes to both the medial and floor walls of the orbit:
fields.explanation: The maxilla forms part of both the medial wall (frontal process) and the floor of the orbit (orbital surface).

fields.text: Which of the following bones forms the floor of the orbit:
fields.explanation: The floor of the orbit is formed primarily by the orbital surface of the maxilla, with contributions from the zygomatic and palatine bones.

fields.text: Which bone contributes to the smallest portion of the orbital wall structure:
fields.explanation: The lacrimal bone is the smallest and most fragile bone contributing to the orbital walls, forming part of the medial wall.

fields.text: The roof of the orbit is primarily formed by which bone:
fields.explanation: The roof of the orbit is formed mainly by the orbital plate of the frontal bone, with a small contribution posteriorly from the lesser wing of the sphenoid.

fields.text: Which statement about the lateral wall of the orbit is TRUE:
fields.option_a: It is the thinnest orbital wall
fields.option_b: It is formed by the zygomatic bone and the greater wing of the sphenoid
fields.option_c: It separates the orbit from the ethmoidal sinus
fields.option_e: It forms the medial boundary of the superior orbital fissure
fields.explanation: The lateral wall of the orbit is formed by the zygomatic bone anteriorly and the greater wing of the sphenoid posteriorly. It is the thickest orbital wall.

fields.text: Which sinus lies directly adjacent to the medial orbital wall:
fields.explanation: The ethmoidal air cells lie directly adjacent to the thin lamina papyracea of the ethmoid bone, which forms most of the medial orbital wall.

fields.text: The optic canal passes through which part of the sphenoid bone:
fields.explanation: The optic canal runs through the lesser wing of the sphenoid bone, transmitting the optic nerve and the ophthalmic artery.

fields.text: Which structure separates the orbital floor from the maxillary sinus:
fields.explanation: The orbital plate of the maxilla forms most of the floor of the orbit and separates the orbit from the underlying maxillary sinus.

fields.text: A blowout fracture of the orbital floor most commonly involves which structure:
fields.explanation: A blowout fracture typically occurs through the thin orbital floor (maxilla), causing herniation of orbital contents into the maxillary sinus.

fields.text: Which of the following forms part of the lacrimal sac fossa:
fields.explanation: The lacrimal sac fossa is formed by the lacrimal bone and the frontal process of the maxilla.

fields.text: The nasolacrimal duct opens into which part of the nasal cavity:
fields.explanation: The nasolacrimal duct drains tears into the inferior meatus of the nasal cavity beneath the inferior turbinate.

fields.text: Which structure forms the posterior lacrimal crest:
fields.explanation: The posterior lacrimal crest is a vertical ridge on the lacrimal bone that helps form the lacrimal sac fossa.

fields.text: The lacrimal sac is located:
fields.option_a: lateral to the medial palpebral ligament
fields.option_b: between the limbs of the medial palpebral ligament
fields.option_c: superior to the medial palpebral ligament
fields.explanation: The lacrimal sac lies within the lacrimal fossa between the anterior and posterior limbs of the medial palpebral ligament.

fields.text: The nasolacrimal duct passes through which bone:
fields.explanation: The nasolacrimal duct passes through a canal in the maxilla and opens into the inferior nasal meatus.

fields.text: The lacrimal sac drains inferiorly into the nasolacrimal duct through which structure:
fields.explanation: The valve of Rosenmüller marks the junction between the lacrimal sac and nasolacrimal duct, helping prevent retrograde flow of tears.

fields.text: The lacrimal sac receives drainage from:
fields.option_c: both superior and inferior canaliculi which join to form a common canaliculus
fields.explanation: The superior and inferior canaliculi usually unite to form a common canaliculus before opening into the lacrimal sac.

fields.text: The lacrimal gland lies within which anatomical fossa:
fields.option_a: lacrimal fossa of the frontal bone
fields.option_c: lacrimal fossa of the zygomatic bone
fields.option_d: lacrimal fossa of the ethmoid bone
fields.option_e: lacrimal fossa of the sphenoid bone
fields.explanation: The lacrimal gland occupies the lacrimal fossa in the superolateral part of the orbit, formed by the frontal bone.

fields.text: Which structure prevents reflux of tears from the nose into the lacrimal sac:
fields.explanation: The valve of Hasner is located at the distal end of the nasolacrimal duct and prevents reflux of nasal secretions into the lacrimal system.

fields.text: Obstruction of the nasolacrimal duct most commonly leads to:
fields.explanation: Obstruction of the nasolacrimal duct can lead to infection and inflammation of the lacrimal sac, known as dacryocystitis.

fields.text: Vitreous is most firmly attached to the:
fields.explanation: The vitreous is most firmly attached to the vitreous base, located around the ora serrata. It also has weaker attachments to the optic disc, macula, and retinal vessels.

fields.text: The vitreous base extends approximately how far anterior and posterior to the ora serrata:
fields.explanation: The vitreous base extends approximately 2 mm anterior and 4 mm posterior to the ora serrata, forming its strongest adhesion zone.

fields.text: The vitreous is composed of approximately what percentage of water:
fields.explanation: The vitreous is composed of about 98–99% water, with the remainder being collagen and hyaluronic acid forming a gel-like matrix.

fields.text: The space between the posterior lens capsule and the anterior face of the vitreous is called:
fields.explanation: Berger’s space lies between the posterior lens capsule and the anterior vitreous face. It is bounded by the hyaloideocapsular ligament of Wieger.

fields.text: Which of the following correctly describes Cloquet’s canal:
fields.option_a: It connects the macula to the optic disc
fields.option_b: It is a remnant of the hyaloid artery running through the vitreous
fields.option_c: It is a canal in the aqueous humor pathway
fields.option_d: It lies between the retina and choroid
fields.option_e: It connects the anterior chamber to the vitreous base
fields.explanation: Cloquet’s canal represents the remnant of the hyaloid artery, extending from the optic disc to the posterior lens capsule.

fields.text: The hyaloideocapsular ligament (of Wieger) represents an adhesion between:
fields.explanation: The hyaloideocapsular ligament (of Wieger) is an annular attachment between the anterior vitreous face and the posterior lens capsule.

fields.text: Which component provides structural stability to the vitreous gel:
fields.explanation: Collagen fibrils form the framework of the vitreous body, while hyaluronic acid maintains hydration and spacing between fibrils.

fields.text: With aging, the vitreous undergoes liquefaction, a process known as:
fields.explanation: Syneresis refers to the aggregation of collagen fibrils and the formation of liquid pockets within the vitreous, commonly seen with age.

fields.text: Posterior vitreous detachment occurs when the vitreous cortex separates from:
fields.option_b: inner limiting membrane of the retina
fields.explanation: Posterior vitreous detachment occurs when the posterior vitreous cortex separates from the inner limiting membrane of the retina.

fields.text: The peripapillary vitreoretinal adhesion is located:
fields.explanation: The peripapillary vitreoretinal adhesion occurs around the optic disc where the vitreous cortex is adherent to the inner limiting membrane.

fields.text: Vitreous is most firmly attached to the:
fields.explanation: The vitreous is most firmly attached to the vitreous base near the ora serrata. It also has secondary adhesions to the optic disc, macula, and along major retinal vessels.

fields.text: The vitreous base represents the attachment between the vitreous and:
fields.explanation: The vitreous base is where the vitreous attaches most strongly to the peripheral retina and the pars plana region of the ciliary body.

fields.text: Which structure within the vitreous represents the remnant of the embryological hyaloid artery:
fields.explanation: Cloquet’s canal (also known as the hyaloid canal) runs from the optic disc to the posterior lens capsule and represents the remnant of the hyaloid artery.

fields.text: The vitreous is least adherent at which of the following locations:
fields.explanation: The vitreous is least adherent at the equatorial region. The strongest adhesion is at the vitreous base near the ora serrata.

fields.text: The vitreous body is surrounded by a condensed layer known as:
fields.explanation: The vitreous cortex is a condensed outer layer of the vitreous body that is in contact with the internal limiting membrane of the retina.

fields.text: Which of the following statements about vitreous syneresis is TRUE:
fields.option_a: It refers to the condensation of collagen fibers with liquefaction of vitreous gel
fields.option_b: It refers to an inflammatory process in the vitreous
fields.option_c: It increases the viscosity of the vitreous humor
fields.option_d: It leads to a denser vitreous base
fields.explanation: Vitreous syneresis refers to age-related aggregation of collagen fibrils and liquefaction of the vitreous gel, predisposing to posterior vitreous detachment.

fields.text: Posterior vitreous detachment occurs when:
fields.option_a: the posterior hyaloid membrane separates from the inner limiting membrane
fields.option_c: the vitreous base detaches from the ora serrata
fields.option_d: the optic nerve separates from the retina
fields.explanation: Posterior vitreous detachment results when the posterior hyaloid membrane separates from the retina’s inner limiting membrane.

fields.text: Which of the following is the earliest site of posterior vitreous detachment:
fields.explanation: The detachment usually starts near the optic disc, where liquefied vitreous enters the potential space between the posterior hyaloid and retina.

fields.text: Which of the following statements regarding the vitreous composition is FALSE:
fields.option_c: It has a higher concentration of proteins than plasma
fields.option_e: It contains ascorbic acid and electrolytes
fields.explanation: The vitreous has a much lower protein concentration than plasma, maintaining its transparency.

fields.text: Which part of the vitreous corresponds to the attachment near the posterior lens capsule:
fields.explanation: Wieger’s ligament is an annular adhesion between the posterior lens capsule and the anterior vitreous, demarcating Berger’s space.

fields.text: The dorsal root of the spinal cord transmits:
fields.explanation: The dorsal root transmits sensory fibres whose cell bodies lie in the dorsal root ganglion. Motor fibres exit via the ventral root.

fields.text: The ventral root of the spinal cord contains:
fields.explanation: The ventral root of the spinal cord carries motor efferent fibres, including somatic motor and preganglionic autonomic neurones.

fields.text: The cell bodies of sensory neurones are located in the:
fields.explanation: Sensory neurone cell bodies lie in the dorsal root ganglia, which are located just outside the spinal cord.

fields.text: Which of the following tracts carries proprioceptive information to the cerebellum:
fields.explanation: The spinocerebellar tracts (anterior and posterior) carry unconscious proprioceptive information to the cerebellum.

fields.text: The lateral spinothalamic tract carries information about:
fields.explanation: The lateral spinothalamic tract transmits pain and temperature sensations from the contralateral side of the body to the thalamus.

fields.text: The corticospinal tract decussates at which level:
fields.explanation: About 80–90% of corticospinal fibres cross at the pyramidal decussation in the medulla to form the lateral corticospinal tract.

fields.text: Which of the following cranial nerves exits from the dorsal aspect of the brainstem:
fields.explanation: The trochlear nerve (CN IV) is unique in exiting the brainstem dorsally, just below the inferior colliculus of the midbrain.

fields.text: Which part of the spinal cord contains preganglionic sympathetic neurones:
fields.explanation: Preganglionic sympathetic neurones are located in the intermediolateral cell column (lateral horn) of spinal segments T1–L2.

fields.text: Preganglionic parasympathetic neurones arise from which spinal cord segments:
fields.explanation: Preganglionic parasympathetic neurones arise from the sacral spinal cord segments S2–S4, as well as brainstem nuclei of cranial nerves III, VII, IX, and X.

fields.text: The dorsal columns (gracile and cuneate fasciculi) carry:
fields.explanation: The dorsal columns transmit fine touch, vibration, and proprioception to the medulla, where they synapse in the gracile and cuneate nuclei.

fields.text: The anterior and posterior ethmoidal nerves are branches of:
fields.explanation: The anterior and posterior ethmoidal nerves are branches of the nasociliary nerve, itself a branch of the ophthalmic division of the trigeminal nerve (CN V1).

fields.text: The nasociliary nerve is a branch of which cranial nerve:
fields.explanation: The nasociliary nerve arises from the ophthalmic division (V1) of the trigeminal nerve and provides sensory innervation to the eye and nasal cavity.

fields.text: Which of the following is NOT a branch of the nasociliary nerve:
fields.explanation: The lacrimal nerve is a separate branch of the ophthalmic nerve (V1), not of the nasociliary nerve. The nasociliary gives off ethmoidal, ciliary, and infratrochlear branches.

fields.text: The infratrochlear nerve supplies sensation to which area:
fields.option_c: medial canthus and side of the nose
fields.explanation: The infratrochlear nerve supplies sensory innervation to the medial canthus, lacrimal sac, and bridge of the nose.

fields.text: Which nerve provides sensory innervation to the cornea:
fields.explanation: The long ciliary nerves, branches of the nasociliary nerve (V1), supply sensory fibres to the cornea.

fields.text: Parasympathetic fibres to the sphincter pupillae reach the eye via:
fields.explanation: Parasympathetic fibres from the ciliary ganglion travel via the short ciliary nerves to supply the sphincter pupillae and ciliary muscle.

fields.text: Which of the following nerves carries sympathetic fibres to the dilator pupillae:
fields.explanation: The long ciliary nerves carry postganglionic sympathetic fibres to the dilator pupillae muscle, originating from the superior cervical ganglion.

fields.text: Which of the following passes through the superior orbital fissure outside the annulus of Zinn:
fields.explanation: The frontal nerve passes through the superior orbital fissure outside the common tendinous ring, dividing into supratrochlear and supraorbital branches.

fields.text: The nasociliary nerve passes through which structure to enter the orbit:
fields.explanation: The nasociliary nerve enters the orbit through the superior orbital fissure within the annulus of Zinn.

fields.text: Which of the following nerves conveys sensation from the nasal septum:
fields.explanation: The posterior ethmoidal nerve, a branch of the nasociliary nerve, provides sensory innervation to the posterior ethmoidal air cells and nasal septum.

fields.text: Which of the following statements is FALSE regarding the CSF:
fields.option_a: it is produced by the modified ependymal cells of the choroid plexus
fields.option_b: blockage of the arachnoid granulations causes communicating hydrocephalus
fields.option_c: it is absorbed mainly through the arachnoid granulations in the superior sagittal sinus
fields.option_d: it flows from the ventricles to the subarachnoid space via the roof of the 3rd ventricle
fields.explanation: CSF flows from the ventricles to the subarachnoid space through the foramina in the roof of the fourth ventricle, not the third.

fields.text: The majority of cerebrospinal fluid is produced by:
fields.explanation: CSF is produced mainly by the choroid plexuses of the lateral, third, and fourth ventricles by modified ependymal cells.

fields.text: CSF exits the fourth ventricle via which openings:
fields.option_e: central canal of the spinal cord only
fields.explanation: CSF leaves the fourth ventricle via the paired lateral foramina of Luschka and the median foramen of Magendie into the subarachnoid space.

fields.text: The aqueduct of Sylvius connects:
fields.option_e: lateral ventricles and fourth ventricle directly
fields.explanation: The cerebral aqueduct (aqueduct of Sylvius) connects the third and fourth ventricles and runs through the midbrain.

fields.text: Obstruction of the cerebral aqueduct leads to:
fields.explanation: Blockage of the cerebral aqueduct prevents CSF flow between the third and fourth ventricles, resulting in non-communicating hydrocephalus.

fields.text: Which of the following structures contains the choroid plexus:
fields.explanation: The choroid plexus is present in all ventricles except the cerebral aqueduct and produces cerebrospinal fluid.

fields.text: Which structure allows CSF reabsorption into the venous system:
fields.explanation: CSF is reabsorbed into the dural venous sinuses through arachnoid granulations, especially into the superior sagittal sinus.

fields.text: The foramen of Monro connects:
fields.explanation: The interventricular foramen (of Monro) connects each lateral ventricle to the third ventricle, allowing CSF flow.

fields.text: Which of the following statements about CSF composition is TRUE:
fields.option_a: it has a higher protein content than plasma
fields.option_b: it is identical in composition to plasma
fields.option_c: it has lower protein and glucose levels than plasma
fields.option_d: it has higher potassium concentration than plasma
fields.explanation: CSF contains lower protein and glucose levels than plasma but is otherwise similar in electrolyte composition.

fields.text: Which cistern contains the termination of the spinal cord:
fields.explanation: The lumbar cistern lies between the termination of the spinal cord (L1–L2) and the end of the dural sac (S2) and contains the cauda equina.

fields.text: Cartilage is found on which bone of the orbit?
fields.explanation: The trochlea of the superior oblique muscle is a fibrocartilaginous pulley attached to the frontal bone. It is the only cartilage found in the normal orbit.

fields.text: Which of the following bones contributes to the floor of the orbit?
fields.explanation: The floor of the orbit is formed by the maxilla (main part), zygomatic bone, and a small part of the palatine bone.

fields.text: The optic canal transmits which structures?
fields.option_d: Trochlear nerve and superior ophthalmic vein
fields.explanation: The optic canal transmits the optic nerve (CN II) and the ophthalmic artery from the cranial cavity into the orbit.

fields.text: The superior orbital fissure lies between which two bones?
fields.explanation: The superior orbital fissure is located between the greater and lesser wings of the sphenoid bone.

fields.text: Which of the following passes through the inferior orbital fissure?
fields.explanation: The inferior orbital fissure transmits the infraorbital nerve and vessels, zygomatic nerve, and inferior ophthalmic vein.

fields.text: Which extraocular muscle passes through the trochlea?
fields.explanation: The tendon of the superior oblique muscle passes through the fibrocartilaginous trochlea before inserting on the superior, posterolateral aspect of the globe.

fields.text: Which muscle originates from the maxilla in the orbit?
fields.explanation: The inferior oblique originates from the orbital surface of the maxilla, just lateral to the lacrimal groove.

fields.text: The common tendinous ring (annulus of Zinn) gives origin to which muscles?
fields.explanation: The annulus of Zinn is the common tendinous ring at the apex of the orbit, giving rise to the four rectus muscles.

fields.text: Which nerve innervates the superior oblique muscle?
fields.explanation: The superior oblique muscle is innervated by the trochlear nerve (CN IV), the only cranial nerve to exit dorsally from the brainstem.

fields.text: The lateral rectus muscle is supplied by which nerve?
fields.explanation: The abducens nerve (CN VI) innervates the lateral rectus muscle, responsible for abduction of the eyeball.

fields.text: All of the following are important to the corneal endothelial pump EXCEPT:
fields.explanation: The corneal endothelium pump maintains corneal deturgescence through ion transport. Key mechanisms include Na/HCO3 cotransport, HCO3/Cl exchange, Na/K ATPase, and Na/H antiport. GLUT3 is a glucose transporter and does not play a role in the endothelial pump.

fields.text: Which of the following best describes the main function of the corneal endothelium?
fields.option_a: Provides tensile strength to the cornea
fields.option_b: Actively pumps ions to maintain corneal dehydration
fields.option_c: Secretes mucin to stabilize the tear film
fields.option_d: Produces collagen fibrils for stromal organization
fields.option_e: Acts as a physical barrier against pathogens
fields.explanation: The corneal endothelium maintains stromal deturgescence (relative dehydration) by actively pumping bicarbonate and sodium ions out of the stroma into the aqueous humor, preventing stromal swelling and maintaining transparency.

fields.text: What is the approximate normal density of corneal endothelial cells in adults?
fields.explanation: Normal adult corneal endothelial cell density is approximately 2500 cells/mm². Values below 1000 cells/mm² are associated with corneal edema due to insufficient pump function.

fields.text: Which statement about corneal endothelial cells is TRUE:
fields.option_b: They regenerate from limbal stem cells
fields.option_c: They enlarge and migrate to cover defects rather than divide
fields.option_d: They are replaced by keratocytes when damaged
fields.option_e: They form tight junctions preventing all fluid movement
fields.explanation: Human corneal endothelial cells are non-replicative. When cell loss occurs, the remaining cells enlarge and migrate to maintain coverage, resulting in decreased density and polymegathism.

fields.text: Loss of corneal transparency in Fuchs endothelial dystrophy is primarily due to:
fields.option_c: Deposition of lipids in Descemet’s membrane
fields.explanation: In Fuchs endothelial dystrophy, endothelial pump failure leads to stromal and epithelial edema. Dysfunction of the Na/K ATPase pump disrupts corneal dehydration and transparency.

fields.text: The corneal endothelium maintains deturgescence mainly by:
fields.option_a: Active transport of bicarbonate and sodium out of the stroma
fields.option_b: Passive diffusion of water into the stroma
fields.option_d: Tight junction formation preventing water passage
fields.explanation: Endothelial cells actively transport Na+ and HCO3− ions toward the aqueous humor, generating osmotic gradients that draw water out of the stroma to maintain corneal clarity.

fields.text: Which of the following transporters is involved at the aqueous interface of the corneal endothelium?
fields.explanation: At the aqueous interface, the Na/HCO3 cotransporter and HCO3/Cl exchanger are active in moving ions into the aqueous humor, supporting the dehydration pump mechanism.

fields.text: Which enzyme is essential for the function of the corneal endothelial pump?
fields.explanation: Carbonic anhydrase catalyzes the reversible conversion of CO2 and H2O into HCO3− and H+, providing bicarbonate ions necessary for the endothelial pump mechanism.

fields.text: Which of the following changes occur in the corneal endothelium with aging?
fields.option_a: Increase in cell density and regularity
fields.option_b: Decrease in cell density with increased polymegathism and pleomorphism
fields.option_c: Proliferation of new endothelial cells from limbal stem cells
fields.option_d: Transformation of endothelial cells into keratocytes
fields.option_e: Thickening of the endothelium due to collagen deposition
fields.explanation: With age, corneal endothelial cells do not regenerate; instead, they enlarge (polymegathism) and change shape (pleomorphism) to cover the same area. Cell density declines gradually from around 4000 to 2500 cells/mm² by adulthood.

fields.text: If the corneal endothelial pump fails completely, what is the most likely clinical consequence?
fields.option_b: Corneal edema and loss of transparency
fields.explanation: Failure of the corneal endothelial pump allows fluid to accumulate in the stroma, increasing corneal thickness and scattering light, leading to corneal edema and loss of transparency. This can occur in Fuchs dystrophy or after endothelial trauma.

fields.text: Northern blotting:
fields.option_a: Allows rapid detection of an infective organism
fields.option_b: Is used to detect DNA molecules
fields.explanation: A Northern blot is similar to a Southern blot except that RNA (usually mRNA) is extracted, separated by gel electrophoresis, and transferred to a filter membrane. It is used to detect the presence and quantity of specific RNA sequences.

fields.text: Which of the following techniques is used to detect specific DNA sequences?
fields.explanation: Southern blotting detects specific DNA sequences by transferring DNA fragments from an agarose gel to a membrane and hybridizing them with a labeled DNA probe.

fields.text: Western blotting is used primarily to detect:
fields.explanation: Western blotting separates proteins by SDS-PAGE and uses antibodies to detect specific proteins, making it a standard method for protein identification and quantification.

fields.text: Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) is primarily used to:
fields.explanation: PCR amplifies specific DNA sequences exponentially using cycles of denaturation, annealing, and extension. It allows detection and analysis of minute amounts of DNA.

fields.text: Which enzyme is essential for PCR?
fields.explanation: Taq polymerase, a heat-stable DNA polymerase from Thermus aquaticus, extends primers during PCR at high temperatures, enabling DNA amplification through repeated thermal cycles.

fields.text: Which laboratory method is used to separate molecules based on size and charge?
fields.explanation: Gel electrophoresis separates nucleic acids or proteins according to size and charge by applying an electric field. Smaller or more charged molecules migrate faster through the gel matrix.

fields.text: What is the role of a probe in blotting techniques?
fields.option_b: To hybridize with a complementary target sequence
fields.explanation: In blotting techniques, labeled nucleic acid probes bind (hybridize) to complementary sequences on the membrane, allowing specific detection of DNA or RNA fragments.

fields.text: Reverse transcriptase PCR (RT-PCR) is used to:
fields.option_a: Amplify RNA by converting it to DNA first
fields.explanation: RT-PCR uses reverse transcriptase to convert RNA into complementary DNA (cDNA), which is then amplified by PCR. It is useful for studying gene expression.

fields.text: Which blotting technique is used to detect specific carbohydrates or glycoconjugates?
fields.explanation: The Eastern blot detects specific post-translational modifications of proteins, such as glycosylation, using probes that bind to carbohydrate moieties.

fields.text: In electrophoresis, which factor primarily determines the rate of molecule migration?
fields.explanation: In electrophoresis, smaller and more highly charged molecules move faster through the gel matrix toward the opposite electrode, depending on the electric field strength and gel composition.

fields.text: Which type of collagen is important in the formation of anchoring fibrils connecting the corneal epithelium to the underlying Bowman's layer?
fields.explanation: Type VII collagen forms anchoring fibrils that link the corneal epithelial basement membrane to Bowman's layer via hemidesmosomes. It provides structural adhesion between the epithelium and the underlying stroma.

fields.text: Which type of collagen is the predominant structural component of the corneal stroma?
fields.explanation: The corneal stroma is primarily composed of Type I collagen fibrils arranged in a regular lamellar pattern that contributes to corneal transparency and tensile strength.

fields.text: Which collagen type helps regulate the diameter of corneal stromal fibrils?
fields.explanation: Type V collagen is co-expressed with Type I in the corneal stroma and regulates fibril diameter, contributing to the uniform lattice arrangement essential for transparency.

fields.text: Which of the following layers of the cornea acts as a basement membrane for the endothelium?
fields.explanation: Descemet’s membrane serves as the basement membrane for the corneal endothelium and thickens with age. It contains Type IV and VIII collagen and provides structural support.

fields.text: Bowman's layer is primarily composed of which type of collagen?
fields.explanation: Bowman’s layer is an acellular condensation of Type I collagen fibers lying beneath the epithelial basement membrane. It contributes to corneal strength and shape maintenance.

fields.text: Which corneal layer is regenerated after injury?
fields.explanation: The corneal epithelium can fully regenerate after injury due to limbal epithelial stem cells. Bowman’s layer does not regenerate, while stromal and endothelial repair is limited.

fields.text: Which of the following is TRUE regarding Descemet’s membrane?
fields.option_a: It is thinner in adults than in children
fields.option_b: It contains Type IV and VIII collagen
fields.option_d: It provides tensile strength to the corneal stroma
fields.option_e: It contains anchoring fibrils for the epithelium
fields.explanation: Descemet’s membrane, secreted by endothelial cells, contains Type IV and VIII collagen. It increases in thickness with age and can regenerate following injury.

fields.text: Which layer of the cornea provides most of its strength and thickness?
fields.explanation: The corneal stroma makes up approximately 90% of the total corneal thickness and provides mechanical strength through regularly arranged collagen lamellae.

fields.text: Which of the following statements about Bowman’s layer is FALSE?
fields.option_c: It lies beneath the epithelial basement membrane
fields.option_d: It is composed mainly of Type I collagen
fields.option_e: It provides structural support to the cornea
fields.explanation: Bowman’s layer does not regenerate after injury. It is an acellular condensation of Type I collagen located beneath the epithelial basement membrane.

fields.text: Which of the following best explains the corneal transparency despite its high collagen content?
fields.option_b: Uniform spacing and diameter of collagen fibrils
fields.explanation: Corneal transparency results from the regular lattice arrangement of collagen fibrils with uniform diameter and spacing, which minimizes light scattering through destructive interference.

fields.text: The corneal endothelium obtains its oxygen from:
fields.explanation: The corneal epithelium receives oxygen from the precorneal tear film, while the endothelium and deep stromal keratocytes obtain oxygen from the aqueous humour.

fields.text: During eye closure (sleep), the main source of oxygen for the corneal epithelium is:
fields.option_b: The palpebral conjunctiva and tarsal vessels
fields.explanation: When the eyes are closed, atmospheric oxygen is cut off and the corneal epithelium receives oxygen mainly from the palpebral conjunctival and tarsal blood vessels.

fields.text: Which metabolic pathway is responsible for most ATP production in the corneal endothelium?
fields.option_b: Aerobic respiration via the Krebs cycle
fields.explanation: The corneal endothelium has abundant mitochondria and relies primarily on aerobic respiration via the Krebs cycle for ATP to maintain its ion pumps and fluid balance.

fields.text: The majority of corneal glucose metabolism occurs through:
fields.explanation: Despite adequate oxygen availability, the cornea metabolizes most glucose anaerobically due to limited vascularity and high metabolic demands of the epithelium.

fields.text: Which byproduct accumulates in the cornea due to anaerobic glycolysis?
fields.explanation: Lactic acid is produced by anaerobic glycolysis in the corneal stroma and epithelium. It diffuses into the aqueous humour and tears to prevent stromal edema.

fields.text: Which statement about corneal oxygen consumption is TRUE?
fields.option_a: It is uniform across all layers
fields.option_b: It is highest in the stroma
fields.option_c: It is highest in the epithelium and endothelium
fields.option_d: It is negligible in the endothelium
fields.option_e: It is dependent on limbal vessels only
fields.explanation: The epithelium and endothelium have the highest metabolic activity in the cornea due to continuous pump and barrier functions, leading to high oxygen demand.

fields.text: Which ion transport mechanism in the endothelium is responsible for maintaining corneal deturgescence?
fields.explanation: The Na+/K+ ATPase pump on the endothelial membrane actively removes ions and fluid from the stroma to the aqueous, maintaining corneal dehydration and transparency.

fields.text: What is the approximate oxygen tension at the corneal surface when the eyes are open?
fields.explanation: When the eyes are open, the oxygen tension at the corneal surface equals atmospheric oxygen (~155 mmHg). During sleep, it drops significantly due to eyelid closure.

fields.text: Which corneal layer is primarily responsible for regulating stromal hydration?
fields.explanation: The corneal endothelium actively pumps fluid from the stroma into the aqueous humour using ion transport mechanisms, maintaining stromal deturgescence and transparency.

fields.text: Which of the following best describes corneal deturgescence?
fields.option_a: Maintenance of optimal stromal hydration for transparency
fields.option_b: Swelling of the cornea due to fluid retention
fields.option_c: Deposition of lactic acid in the stroma
fields.option_d: Reduction of endothelial permeability to water
fields.explanation: Corneal deturgescence refers to the active maintenance of a specific level of stromal hydration (about 78%) required for optimal transparency and refractive performance.

fields.text: Which of the following attaches cells to the extracellular matrix and to ligands on other cells?
fields.explanation: Integrins are heterodimeric transmembrane proteins that link the cytoskeleton to extracellular matrix components such as fibronectin and laminin. They require magnesium and calcium for activity.

fields.text: Integrins are composed of how many subunits?
fields.option_c: One alpha and one beta subunit
fields.option_e: Two alpha and one beta subunit
fields.explanation: Integrins are heterodimeric receptors composed of one alpha and one beta subunit, which together determine ligand specificity and binding affinity.

fields.text: Which of the following extracellular matrix proteins binds to integrins and facilitates cell adhesion?
fields.explanation: Fibronectin is a high-molecular-weight glycoprotein that binds integrins, collagen, and fibrin, facilitating cell adhesion, migration, and wound healing.

fields.text: Which of the following best describes the role of hemidesmosomes in corneal epithelium?
fields.option_a: They connect epithelial cells to each other
fields.option_b: They anchor epithelial cells to the basement membrane
fields.option_c: They form tight junctions between epithelial cells
fields.explanation: Hemidesmosomes anchor basal epithelial cells to the basement membrane through integrins and Type VII collagen, providing mechanical stability to the corneal surface.

fields.text: Which collagen type is directly associated with anchoring fibrils in hemidesmosomes?
fields.explanation: Type VII collagen forms anchoring fibrils that link the epithelial basement membrane to the underlying stroma and play a key role in hemidesmosomal adhesion.

fields.text: Which junction provides a barrier to paracellular fluid movement in the corneal epithelium?
fields.explanation: Tight junctions (zonula occludens) between corneal epithelial cells prevent the passage of solutes and fluids, maintaining corneal barrier integrity.

fields.text: Which of the following connects actin filaments of one cell to those of another?
fields.explanation: Adherens junctions (zonula adherens) connect actin filaments of adjacent epithelial cells via cadherins and catenins, providing mechanical linkage.

fields.text: Gap junctions in the cornea primarily function to:
fields.option_a: Anchor the cells to the basement membrane
fields.option_b: Allow electrical and metabolic coupling between cells
fields.option_c: Provide mechanical stability to the epithelium
fields.option_d: Prevent diffusion of solutes between cells
fields.option_e: Bind the cornea to the sclera
fields.explanation: Gap junctions formed by connexin proteins permit direct intercellular exchange of ions and small molecules, allowing metabolic and electrical coordination in the cornea.

fields.text: Which ion is essential for integrin-mediated adhesion?
fields.explanation: Integrins require divalent cations, particularly magnesium and calcium, to bind ligands such as fibronectin and laminin effectively.

fields.text: Which of the following structures contains cadherins as the primary adhesion molecule?
fields.explanation: Desmosomes utilize cadherins (desmogleins and desmocollins) as calcium-dependent adhesion molecules that link intermediate filaments between cells.

fields.text: The following are true about inositol triphosphate (IP3) EXCEPT:
fields.option_a: It increases the intracellular concentration of calcium
fields.option_b: It is increased by activation of α1-adrenoreceptors
fields.option_c: It acts as a second messenger
fields.option_e: It stimulates release of Ca²⁺ from the endoplasmic reticulum
fields.explanation: IP3 is a second messenger generated following activation of α1-adrenoreceptors. It causes calcium release from intracellular stores, resulting in smooth muscle contraction, not relaxation.

fields.text: Which enzyme catalyzes the formation of IP3 and diacylglycerol (DAG)?
fields.explanation: Phospholipase C hydrolyzes phosphatidylinositol 4,5-bisphosphate (PIP2) to form IP3 and DAG, both of which act as second messengers in signal transduction.

fields.text: What is the main function of diacylglycerol (DAG) in cell signaling?
fields.option_a: To release calcium from intracellular stores
fields.explanation: DAG remains within the plasma membrane and activates protein kinase C (PKC), which phosphorylates target proteins to modulate cellular activity.

fields.text: Which G-protein subtype activates phospholipase C?
fields.explanation: Gq proteins activate phospholipase C, leading to the production of IP3 and DAG from PIP2 and increasing intracellular calcium levels.

fields.text: Which of the following second messengers is associated with β-adrenergic receptor stimulation?
fields.explanation: β-adrenergic receptors are coupled to Gs proteins, which activate adenylyl cyclase to increase cAMP levels, activating protein kinase A (PKA).

fields.text: Which enzyme is directly activated by cyclic AMP (cAMP)?
fields.explanation: Protein kinase A (PKA) is the primary effector enzyme activated by cAMP, leading to phosphorylation of specific intracellular proteins.

fields.text: Nitric oxide (NO) activates which intracellular enzyme?
fields.explanation: Nitric oxide diffuses into target cells and activates soluble guanylyl cyclase, leading to increased cyclic GMP (cGMP) production and smooth muscle relaxation.

fields.text: Which of the following is NOT a typical action of cyclic GMP (cGMP)?
fields.explanation: cGMP causes smooth muscle relaxation and vasodilation by decreasing intracellular calcium concentration and activating protein kinase G.

fields.text: Which enzyme breaks down cAMP into AMP, terminating its signaling?
fields.explanation: Phosphodiesterase (PDE) hydrolyzes cyclic AMP to AMP, thereby reducing PKA activity and terminating the cAMP signaling pathway.

fields.text: The IP3/DAG pathway leads to smooth muscle contraction primarily by:
fields.option_c: Increasing calcium release from the endoplasmic reticulum
fields.explanation: IP3 binds to receptors on the endoplasmic reticulum, triggering calcium release, which increases myosin light chain phosphorylation and smooth muscle contraction.

fields.text: The following are true about inositol triphosphate (IP3) EXCEPT:
fields.option_a: It increases the intracellular concentration of calcium
fields.option_b: It is increased by activation of α1-adrenoreceptors
fields.option_c: It acts as a second messenger
fields.option_e: It binds to receptors on the endoplasmic reticulum
fields.explanation: IP3 is produced via phospholipase C activation following α1-adrenoceptor stimulation. It increases intracellular calcium by binding to receptors on the endoplasmic reticulum, leading to smooth muscle contraction rather than relaxation.

fields.text: Which molecule remains membrane-bound after phosphatidylinositol 4,5-bisphosphate (PIP2) is cleaved by phospholipase C?
fields.explanation: Diacylglycerol (DAG) remains embedded in the plasma membrane after PIP2 cleavage and activates protein kinase C, while IP3 diffuses into the cytoplasm.

fields.text: Which of the following statements about phospholipase C (PLC) is TRUE?
fields.option_a: It is inhibited by Gq proteins
fields.option_c: It catalyzes the breakdown of PIP2 to IP3 and DAG
fields.explanation: Phospholipase C catalyzes the hydrolysis of phosphatidylinositol 4,5-bisphosphate (PIP2) to form IP3 and DAG, both of which act as important second messengers.

fields.text: Which of the following second messenger systems is activated by α1-adrenergic receptors?
fields.explanation: α1-Adrenergic receptors are coupled to Gq proteins that activate phospholipase C, leading to the generation of IP3 and DAG, which increase intracellular calcium and activate protein kinase C.

fields.text: Which ion directly triggers contraction in smooth muscle cells?
fields.explanation: Increased intracellular calcium binds to calmodulin, activating myosin light chain kinase (MLCK) and promoting smooth muscle contraction.

fields.text: The enzyme that converts ATP to cyclic AMP (cAMP) is:
fields.explanation: Adenylyl cyclase is activated by Gs proteins and converts ATP into cyclic AMP (cAMP), which serves as a key second messenger in many signaling pathways.

fields.text: Which of the following second messengers is formed by the action of nitric oxide (NO)?
fields.explanation: Nitric oxide activates soluble guanylyl cyclase, leading to the conversion of GTP to cyclic GMP (cGMP), which causes smooth muscle relaxation.

fields.text: Which of the following drugs acts by increasing cyclic GMP concentration in smooth muscle?
fields.explanation: Sildenafil inhibits phosphodiesterase type 5 (PDE-5), preventing the breakdown of cGMP and thus promoting smooth muscle relaxation and vasodilation.

fields.text: In smooth muscle, activation of protein kinase C (PKC) primarily results from the accumulation of:
fields.explanation: Diacylglycerol (DAG) activates protein kinase C (PKC), which phosphorylates proteins involved in smooth muscle contraction and other cellular processes.

fields.text: In the IP3/DAG pathway, calcium released from the endoplasmic reticulum amplifies signaling by activating:
fields.explanation: Calcium released by IP3 binds to calmodulin, forming a complex that activates calcium/calmodulin-dependent kinases, which phosphorylate target proteins.

fields.text: Which is FALSE regarding the composition of the aqueous humour compared to plasma?
fields.option_a: There is a higher concentration of sodium in the aqueous
fields.option_b: There is a higher concentration of lactate in aqueous
fields.option_c: There is a lower concentration of albumin in the aqueous
fields.option_d: There is a higher concentration of ascorbate in aqueous
fields.option_e: There is a lower concentration of glucose in aqueous
fields.explanation: The aqueous humour has a lower concentration of most constituents compared to plasma, except for lactate, ascorbate, and chloride. Sodium concentration is actually slightly lower, not higher, than in plasma.

fields.text: Which ion is actively secreted into the posterior chamber during aqueous humour formation?
fields.explanation: Aqueous secretion by the ciliary epithelium depends on active Na⁺ transport via Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase, which creates an osmotic gradient driving water movement into the posterior chamber.

fields.text: Which enzyme is most directly involved in the secretion of aqueous humour?
fields.explanation: Carbonic anhydrase catalyzes the reversible conversion of CO₂ and H₂O into bicarbonate and protons, providing the bicarbonate ions required for aqueous humour secretion by the ciliary processes.

fields.text: Which structure produces the aqueous humour?
fields.option_b: Ciliary processes of the ciliary body
fields.explanation: The non-pigmented ciliary epithelium in the ciliary processes actively secretes aqueous humour into the posterior chamber.

fields.text: Aqueous humour drains primarily through which pathway?
fields.explanation: About 80–90% of aqueous outflow occurs via the trabecular meshwork and Schlemm’s canal, which leads to the episcleral veins.

fields.text: Which of the following is TRUE about aqueous humour compared to plasma?
fields.option_c: It has higher lactate and ascorbate concentrations
fields.explanation: Aqueous humour contains higher levels of lactate, ascorbate, and chloride, but lower protein, glucose, and sodium than plasma.

fields.text: Which of the following statements regarding aqueous humour flow is CORRECT?
fields.option_a: It flows from anterior chamber to posterior chamber
fields.option_b: It is secreted by pigmented ciliary epithelium
fields.option_c: It flows from posterior chamber through the pupil to the anterior chamber
fields.option_d: It drains mainly via the uveoscleral route
fields.option_e: It does not depend on active ion transport
fields.explanation: Aqueous humour is secreted into the posterior chamber and flows through the pupil into the anterior chamber before draining mainly through the trabecular meshwork.

fields.text: Which drug class decreases aqueous humour secretion by inhibiting carbonic anhydrase?
fields.explanation: Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors such as acetazolamide and dorzolamide reduce aqueous humour production by inhibiting bicarbonate formation in the ciliary epithelium.

fields.text: Which drug increases aqueous outflow through the uveoscleral pathway?
fields.explanation: Prostaglandin analogues like latanoprost increase uveoscleral outflow by remodeling the extracellular matrix of the ciliary muscle.

fields.text: The rate of aqueous humour production is approximately:
