fields.text: A patient with a defect in MHC class I expression is most likely to have which immunological problem?
fields.option_b: Impaired CD8+ cytotoxic T cell response
fields.option_c: Impaired CD4+ helper T cell activation
fields.explanation: MHC class I deficiency results in defective activation of CD8+ cytotoxic T lymphocytes, leading to increased viral susceptibility.

fields.text: Which of the following statements regarding MHC molecules is FALSE?
fields.option_a: MHC class I presents peptides derived from endogenous proteins
fields.option_b: MHC class II presents peptides derived from exogenous proteins
fields.option_c: MHC class I is expressed only on antigen-presenting cells
fields.option_d: MHC class II is expressed on macrophages, dendritic cells and B cells
fields.option_e: CD4+ T cells recognise MHC class II molecules
fields.explanation: MHC class I molecules are expressed on all nucleated cells, not just antigen-presenting cells. This allows CD8+ T cells to detect intracellular infection or malignancy.

fields.text: Fluorescein dye absorbs light of which wavelength?
fields.explanation: Fluorescein is excited by blue light at wavelengths between 465–490 nm. The emitted light is yellow-green, which is captured through a barrier filter in angiography.

fields.text: What colour of light is emitted by fluorescein after excitation?
fields.explanation: When excited by blue light, fluorescein emits yellow-green fluorescence (around 520–530 nm), which is captured through a yellow-green barrier filter.

fields.text: Which filter is used in fundus cameras to isolate the emitted fluorescence from fluorescein?
fields.explanation: The barrier (or emission) filter blocks blue excitation light and allows only the yellow-green fluorescence emitted by fluorescein to be recorded.

fields.text: Which of the following best describes the optical principle of fluorescein angiography?
fields.option_a: Absorption and emission at the same wavelength
fields.option_b: Excitation by blue light and emission of yellow-green fluorescence
fields.option_c: Excitation by red light and emission of blue light
fields.option_d: Reflection of incident light by retinal vessels
fields.option_e: Transmission of near-infrared light through retinal pigment epithelium
fields.explanation: Fluorescein angiography relies on excitation by blue light and emission of yellow-green fluorescence, highlighting retinal and choroidal circulation.

fields.text: Which of the following statements about fluorescein dye is TRUE?
fields.option_a: It is highly protein-bound in the blood
fields.option_b: It diffuses freely through intact retinal capillaries
fields.option_c: It remains confined within the retinal circulation
fields.option_d: It is metabolized by the retina before excretion
fields.option_e: It is excreted primarily via the lungs
fields.explanation: Fluorescein is approximately 80% protein-bound in plasma and is excreted via the kidneys. It leaks from damaged but not intact retinal capillaries.

fields.text: In fluorescein angiography, which phase corresponds to the choroidal circulation filling?
fields.explanation: The choroidal flush occurs first, as the choroidal circulation fills before the central retinal artery during fluorescein angiography.

fields.text: Which structure acts as the main barrier to fluorescein leakage in the eye?
fields.explanation: The retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) forms a key part of the outer blood-retinal barrier, preventing fluorescein leakage from the choriocapillaris into the retina.

fields.text: Which of the following is a recognized side effect of intravenous fluorescein?
fields.explanation: Transient nausea and vomiting are the most common side effects of intravenous fluorescein. Rarely, urticaria and anaphylaxis can occur.

fields.text: Which of the following artefacts is most likely if fluorescein angiography is performed too early after injection?
fields.explanation: If imaging starts too early, the choroidal flush and arterial phases may appear overexposed due to incomplete mixing of the dye in circulation.

fields.text: Which of the following best describes the emission and excitation peaks of fluorescein?
fields.explanation: Fluorescein’s excitation peak is around 465–490 nm (blue light), and it emits yellow-green light at 520–530 nm.

fields.text: When do the majority of flap folds occur after laser-assisted in situ keratomileusis (LASIK)?
fields.explanation: More than half of flap folds occur within the first postoperative day, and most occur within the first week. Visually significant folds should be corrected within 24 hours to prevent visual distortion.

fields.text: Which of the following is the most likely cause of macrostriae after LASIK?
fields.explanation: Macrostriae are large full-thickness folds caused by slippage or poor repositioning of the LASIK flap, resulting in visual distortion that may require flap relift and repositioning.

fields.text: Which of the following statements about LASIK flap complications is TRUE?
fields.option_a: Microstriae are usually symptomatic and require urgent treatment
fields.option_b: Macrostriae often resolve spontaneously without intervention
fields.option_c: Flap dislocation is most likely to occur within the first 24 hours after surgery
fields.option_d: Epithelial ingrowth occurs within the first 24 hours
fields.option_e: Buttonholes occur due to incomplete stromal drying before flap creation
fields.explanation: Flap dislocations typically occur within the first day after LASIK, especially if the patient rubs the eye or experiences trauma before flap adhesion stabilizes.

fields.text: Which of the following findings indicates a visually significant LASIK flap fold requiring surgical intervention?
fields.option_a: Peripheral microstriae outside the optical zone
fields.option_e: Fine folds confined to flap edge
fields.explanation: Macrostriae that cross the visual axis produce irregular astigmatism and decreased vision. These should be corrected by lifting and refloating the flap.

fields.text: What is the most likely cause of buttonhole flap formation during LASIK?
fields.explanation: Buttonhole flaps occur when suction is lost during the microkeratome pass, causing the blade to skip or cut irregularly, leaving a central defect in the flap.

fields.text: Which of the following LASIK complications is MOST likely to cause a diffuse granular haze in the interface during the early postoperative period?
fields.explanation: Diffuse lamellar keratitis (DLK) presents as a granular inflammatory haze within the flap interface in the first few days post-LASIK and requires topical corticosteroids.

fields.text: Epithelial ingrowth following LASIK typically presents how long after surgery?
fields.explanation: Epithelial ingrowth usually occurs within several weeks following LASIK, often at the flap edge or during enhancement surgery. It appears as grey-white nests at the interface.

fields.text: Which factor increases the risk of postoperative LASIK flap folds?
fields.explanation: Steep corneas increase the likelihood of flap slippage and subsequent macrostriae formation due to curvature mismatch after repositioning.

fields.text: Which of the following best describes microstriae after LASIK?
fields.option_b: Fine, shallow surface folds without visual significance
fields.explanation: Microstriae are fine superficial folds of the flap that are usually asymptomatic and often resolve spontaneously without intervention.

fields.text: What is the recommended management for visually significant LASIK flap macrostriae?
fields.option_c: Flap relift and irrigation with refloat
fields.explanation: Significant macrostriae should be corrected promptly by relifting the flap, irrigating the interface, and refloating to ensure proper alignment and smooth optical surface.

fields.text: Which statement is TRUE regarding prisms?
fields.option_a: Convergence insufficiency is the commonest therapeutic use of prisms
fields.option_b: The Maddox rod is an objective measure of squint
fields.option_c: The prism cover test is a subjective measure of squint
fields.option_d: Prisms can be stacked one on top of the other to produce an additive effect
fields.option_e: Prisms are most commonly used to measure cyclotropia
fields.explanation: Convergence insufficiency is the most frequent therapeutic indication for base-in prisms. The prism cover test is an objective method of measuring deviation, while the Maddox rod is subjective.

fields.text: In the prism cover test, which of the following best describes how prisms are used?
fields.option_a: They are stacked for increased power
fields.option_b: They are placed with the base toward the deviation
fields.option_c: They are used with the Maddox rod for subjective testing
fields.option_e: They are used to produce fusion in non-fusible diplopia
fields.explanation: During the prism cover test, the prism is placed with its base toward the direction of deviation to neutralize the movement and measure the angle of strabismus.

fields.text: Which test provides an objective assessment of ocular deviation?
fields.explanation: The prism cover test objectively measures the angle of deviation by observing eye movements when alternating the cover. The Maddox rod and Worth four-dot are subjective tests.

fields.text: Why should prisms not be stacked directly on top of each other in the same meridian?
fields.option_b: It alters the angle of incidence and reduces accuracy
fields.option_e: It causes optical distortion at the edges
fields.explanation: Stacking prisms alters the angle of incidence between surfaces, making their effects non-additive and leading to measurement error. Prisms should be combined geometrically, not physically stacked.

fields.text: Which of the following best describes the optical principle of a prism?
fields.option_a: It diverges light toward its base
fields.option_b: It converges light toward its base
fields.option_c: It bends light toward its apex
fields.option_d: It refracts light toward its base
fields.option_e: It diffracts light into its component colors
fields.explanation: A prism refracts light toward its base, making the image appear displaced toward its apex. This property is used both diagnostically and therapeutically in ophthalmology.

fields.text: What is the most common therapeutic indication for prescribing base-in prisms?
fields.explanation: Base-in prisms are used in convergence insufficiency to relieve diplopia and reduce the need for excessive convergence effort at near.

fields.text: In measuring vertical deviations with prisms, which of the following is TRUE?
fields.option_a: Prisms are placed base-in or base-out
fields.option_b: The stronger prism is placed before the fixing eye
fields.option_c: Base-up and base-down prisms are used depending on the hypertropic eye
fields.option_d: Prisms should be stacked in opposite orientations for accuracy
fields.option_e: They cannot be used for deviations less than 5 prism diopters
fields.explanation: Vertical deviations are measured with base-up or base-down prisms depending on which eye is hypertropic, and the deviation is quantified when eye movement is neutralized.

fields.text: Which unit is used to measure the power of a prism?
fields.explanation: Prism power is expressed in prism diopters (Δ), where 1Δ deflects a light ray by 1 cm at a distance of 1 meter. It is a practical clinical unit for strabismus measurement.

fields.text: Which of the following statements about the Maddox rod test is TRUE?
fields.option_a: It provides an objective measure of deviation
fields.option_b: It dissociates the eyes to prevent fusion
fields.option_c: It measures deviation at both distance and near simultaneously
fields.option_d: It is mainly used for cyclotropia measurement
fields.option_e: It cannot be used for small phorias
fields.explanation: The Maddox rod test dissociates binocular vision by creating dissimilar images in each eye, providing a subjective assessment of ocular alignment.

fields.text: Which statement about the prism cover test is CORRECT?
fields.option_a: It relies on patient response to quantify deviation
fields.option_b: It is performed with both eyes open under binocular fixation
fields.option_c: It cannot be used for microtropia
fields.option_d: It is more accurate for small deviations than the Maddox rod
fields.explanation: The prism cover test is performed under binocular fixation conditions to determine the prism strength that neutralizes ocular deviation, providing an objective measurement.

fields.text: Novices at indirect ophthalmoscopy tend to move closer to the patient than the optimal examining distance to:
fields.explanation: Moving closer increases the image size, brightness, and clarity but reduces the field of view and requires a larger pupil to maintain alignment.

fields.text: In indirect ophthalmoscopy, how does decreasing the working distance affect the image?
fields.option_a: Increases magnification but reduces field of view
fields.option_b: Decreases magnification and field of view
fields.option_c: Increases field of view and depth of focus
fields.option_d: Increases field of view but reduces brightness
fields.option_e: Reduces both image size and brightness
fields.explanation: Decreasing the working distance increases image magnification and brightness but reduces the overall field of view and depth of focus.

fields.text: Which of the following best describes the image formed in indirect ophthalmoscopy?
fields.explanation: The image formed in indirect ophthalmoscopy is real, inverted, and laterally reversed because it is formed between the condensing lens and the observer’s eye.

fields.text: Compared to direct ophthalmoscopy, indirect ophthalmoscopy provides:
fields.option_a: Higher magnification but smaller field of view
fields.option_b: Lower magnification but larger field of view
fields.option_c: Equal magnification and field of view
fields.option_d: Higher magnification and same field of view
fields.explanation: Indirect ophthalmoscopy offers a wider field of view with stereopsis but at lower magnification compared to direct ophthalmoscopy.

fields.text: Which of the following lenses provides the greatest image magnification in indirect ophthalmoscopy?
fields.explanation: A 14D condensing lens provides greater image magnification but a smaller field of view compared to higher-powered lenses like 20D or 28D.

fields.text: What is the main optical disadvantage of moving too close to the patient during indirect ophthalmoscopy?
fields.explanation: As the examiner moves closer, the field of view narrows, making peripheral retinal visualization more difficult, especially in small pupils.

fields.text: Which factor primarily determines the field of view during binocular indirect ophthalmoscopy?
fields.option_c: Distance between condensing lens and patient’s eye
fields.explanation: The field of view is primarily determined by the dioptric power of the condensing lens: higher-powered lenses (e.g., 28D) give a wider field but smaller image.

fields.text: Which of the following best describes the image orientation in indirect ophthalmoscopy compared to direct ophthalmoscopy?
fields.explanation: Indirect ophthalmoscopy produces an image that is inverted and laterally reversed, unlike direct ophthalmoscopy, which provides an upright, virtual image.

fields.text: What is the approximate magnification of a 20D condensing lens in binocular indirect ophthalmoscopy?
fields.explanation: A 20D lens typically provides around 3x magnification with a 45–50° field of view, which balances clarity and peripheral visualization effectively.

fields.text: Which of the following correctly matches lens power to field of view in indirect ophthalmoscopy?
fields.option_a: Higher power lens gives smaller field of view
fields.option_b: Higher power lens gives larger field of view
fields.option_c: Lower power lens gives larger field of view
fields.option_d: Field of view is independent of lens power
fields.option_e: Field of view depends only on examiner’s distance
fields.explanation: Higher-powered condensing lenses (e.g., 28D or 30D) provide a wider field of view and smaller image size, while lower-powered lenses offer greater magnification.

fields.text: If a patient with untreated congenital esotropia is tested with the Hess chart, when the glasses are reversed and the test is repeated, which one of the following statements is true?
fields.option_a: The position of the lights on the chart will remain the same
fields.option_b: The position of the lights on the chart will reverse, and the distance will remain the same
fields.option_c: The position of the lights on the screen will reverse, and the distance between them will increase
fields.option_d: The position of the lights on the chart will not reverse, and the distance will increase
fields.explanation: Due to suppression in congenital esotropia, the Hess chart cannot be performed effectively. It is designed for acquired strabismus, where diplopia is present. Patients with congenital strabismus perceive only one light.

fields.text: What is the main purpose of the Hess chart in strabismus evaluation?
fields.option_b: To quantify ocular deviation in prism diopters
fields.option_c: To differentiate between restrictive and paretic squints
fields.explanation: The Hess chart is used to identify and differentiate paretic from restrictive ocular motility disorders by plotting underaction and overaction patterns of extraocular muscles.

fields.text: In a Hess chart, how is the affected eye identified?
fields.option_a: By smaller field of plotted points
fields.option_b: By larger field of plotted points
fields.option_c: By reversed orientation of plotted field
fields.option_e: By denser clustering of points near the center
fields.explanation: The eye with a smaller plotted field represents the affected eye, as its movement is limited due to paresis or restriction, while the contralateral field appears expanded due to overaction.

fields.text: When performing the Hess chart, what will happen if the red-green glasses are swapped between eyes in a patient with acquired sixth nerve palsy?
fields.option_b: The fields will reverse sides horizontally
fields.option_c: The smaller field will remain in the same position
fields.option_e: No change will occur due to suppression
fields.explanation: Swapping the red-green filters reverses the visual input between eyes, so the plotted fields will switch sides horizontally while maintaining their relative size and shape.

fields.text: Why is the Hess chart not useful in congenital strabismus?
fields.option_b: Because suppression prevents perception of diplopia
fields.option_c: Because it measures only horizontal deviations
fields.option_e: Because it depends on binocular fusion amplitude
fields.explanation: In congenital strabismus, suppression prevents the patient from perceiving diplopia, so only one light is seen and the Hess test cannot be performed accurately.

fields.text: What does an enlarged field in the Hess chart of one eye indicate?
fields.explanation: An enlarged field on the Hess chart corresponds to the secondary deviation when plotting with the unaffected (fixing) eye, as the deviation appears larger in the non-paretic eye due to Hering’s law.

fields.text: Which of the following best differentiates a restrictive from a paralytic squint on Hess chart interpretation?
fields.option_a: Limitation of movement in both fields in the same direction
fields.option_b: Increased field size in the paretic eye
fields.option_d: Enlarged field in the affected eye
fields.option_e: Suppression of one field on reversal
fields.explanation: In restrictive squints, movement limitation is seen in both eyes in the same direction of gaze, unlike in paralytic squints where only the affected eye shows underaction.

fields.text: In Hess chart plotting, the test should ideally be performed:
fields.option_a: In a fully dark room without fixation targets
fields.option_b: With both eyes open and alternating fixation
fields.option_c: At a fixed distance using colored goggles to dissociate the eyes
fields.option_d: Monocularly with occlusion of one eye
fields.option_e: With head tilted toward the fixing eye
fields.explanation: The Hess test is performed in a dimly lit room using red-green goggles to dissociate the two eyes, ensuring each eye views only its designated target field.

fields.text: Which muscle is most likely underacting if the plotted points are displaced downward and inward on the Hess chart?
fields.explanation: Displacement of plotted points downward and inward indicates underaction of the superior oblique muscle, which primarily depresses and intorts the eye in adduction.

fields.text: A patient shows a smaller field on the left eye and a larger field on the right eye on the Hess chart. Which eye is affected?
fields.explanation: The smaller field corresponds to the affected eye because of reduced movement range due to muscle paresis. The larger field (secondary deviation) occurs in the non-affected eye.

fields.text: A patient presenting with decreased visual acuity in the setting of suspected orbital cellulitis must be evaluated with which of the following tests?
fields.explanation: Decreased vision in orbital cellulitis suggests possible orbital apex involvement or subperiosteal abscess formation. Urgent contrast-enhanced CT is required to assess the extent of infection and any drainable collections.

fields.text: What is the most common source of infection in orbital cellulitis?
fields.explanation: Ethmoid sinusitis is the most common predisposing cause of orbital cellulitis due to the thin lamina papyracea, which allows spread of infection into the orbit.

fields.text: Which of the following features differentiates orbital cellulitis from preseptal cellulitis?
fields.explanation: Pain on eye movement, ophthalmoplegia, and decreased vision are key features of orbital cellulitis. Preseptal cellulitis involves only the tissues anterior to the orbital septum and spares ocular motility.

fields.text: In a child with suspected orbital cellulitis and limited ocular movement, what is the next best step in management?
fields.option_c: Urgent contrast-enhanced CT orbit and sinuses
fields.explanation: Limited eye movements and proptosis suggest post-septal spread. Urgent contrast-enhanced CT of orbit and sinuses is essential to identify abscess formation and prevent vision-threatening complications.

fields.text: Which of the following is NOT a typical causative organism in orbital cellulitis?
fields.explanation: Pseudomonas aeruginosa is uncommon in orbital cellulitis except in immunocompromised patients or following trauma. The commonest organisms are Staphylococcus, Streptococcus, and Haemophilus (in children).

fields.text: Which imaging modality is preferred for differentiating orbital cellulitis from cavernous sinus thrombosis?
fields.option_b: MRI with contrast and MR venography
fields.option_e: Plain X-ray of skull and sinuses
fields.explanation: MRI with contrast and MR venography is preferred to assess venous structures and differentiate orbital cellulitis from cavernous sinus thrombosis, as it provides superior soft-tissue detail.

fields.text: Which of the following clinical findings most strongly suggests subperiosteal abscess formation in orbital cellulitis?
fields.explanation: Localized proptosis with restricted eye movement indicates a subperiosteal abscess, often secondary to ethmoid sinusitis. CT imaging confirms diagnosis and extent of collection.

fields.text: In orbital cellulitis, which of the following signs would prompt urgent surgical drainage?
fields.option_b: Minimal proptosis and full ocular movement
fields.option_c: Decreased vision and relative afferent pupillary defect
fields.option_e: Pain on palpation of sinuses only
fields.explanation: Decreased vision and a relative afferent pupillary defect indicate optic nerve compression or orbital apex involvement — both require urgent decompression or drainage of abscess.

fields.text: What is the initial management of a child with orbital cellulitis and normal visual acuity?
fields.option_b: IV antibiotics and ENT referral for sinus drainage
fields.explanation: Initial management includes IV broad-spectrum antibiotics covering Staphylococcus, Streptococcus, and anaerobes, along with ENT assessment for sinus drainage. Surgical decompression is indicated only for abscess or vision loss.

fields.text: Which of the following complications is most feared in untreated orbital cellulitis?
fields.explanation: Cavernous sinus thrombosis is a life-threatening complication of orbital cellulitis due to venous drainage connections between the orbit and cavernous sinus. Prompt imaging and IV antibiotics are essential.

fields.text: A cross-cylinder is a type of:
fields.explanation: The cross-cylinder, or Jackson’s cross-cylinder (JCC), is a toric or sphero-cylindrical lens combining equal but opposite cylindrical powers at right angles. It is used to refine astigmatic correction during subjective refraction.

fields.text: Which of the following best describes the optical property of a Jackson cross-cylinder lens?
fields.option_a: It has two equal spherical powers of opposite sign
fields.option_b: It has two cylindrical powers of equal magnitude and opposite sign at right angles
fields.option_c: It has a single cylindrical surface
fields.option_d: It acts as a prism with equal base-in and base-out components
fields.option_e: It produces equal magnification in all meridians
fields.explanation: A Jackson cross-cylinder (JCC) has two cylindrical components of equal magnitude and opposite sign at right angles, allowing comparison of two astigmatic corrections during axis and power refinement.

fields.text: During subjective refraction, the cross-cylinder is used primarily to:
fields.option_b: Determine best visual acuity in the distance
fields.explanation: The Jackson cross-cylinder is used to refine both the axis and power of cylindrical correction by comparing clarity between two lens positions rotated around the optical axis.

fields.text: When refining cylinder axis using the Jackson cross-cylinder, the handle of the JCC is aligned:
fields.option_e: With the principal meridian of the eye
fields.explanation: During axis refinement, the handle of the JCC is held perpendicular to the cylinder axis, allowing the patient to compare two equal and opposite astigmatic corrections for clarity.

fields.text: When refining cylinder power using the Jackson cross-cylinder, the handle is aligned:
fields.explanation: When refining power, the handle of the JCC is held parallel to the cylinder axis, comparing more or less astigmatic correction depending on which lens position gives clearer vision.

fields.text: In a Jackson cross-cylinder with ±0.25 D, what is the total difference in power between the two positions when flipped?
fields.explanation: Flipping a ±0.25 D Jackson cross-cylinder produces a total difference of 0.50 D between the two positions, as the axes alternate between +0.25 D and −0.25 D in perpendicular meridians.

fields.text: In retinoscopy, the term ‘neutral point’ refers to:
fields.option_a: The point where the light reflex reverses direction
fields.option_b: The distance where no movement of the reflex is observed
fields.option_c: The midpoint between with- and against-movement zones
fields.option_d: The distance where the brightest reflex is observed
fields.explanation: The neutral point in retinoscopy occurs when the patient's far point coincides with the observer’s plane, resulting in no movement of the retinal reflex — indicating neutralization.

fields.text: When performing retinoscopy at 50 cm, what working distance correction should be subtracted from the final prescription?
fields.explanation: A working distance of 50 cm corresponds to a vergence of 2.00 D (1/0.5 = 2.00). This value is subtracted from the gross retinoscopy finding to obtain the final prescription.

fields.text: In retinoscopy, 'with' movement of the reflex indicates:
fields.option_a: The patient is myopic beyond the neutral point
fields.option_b: The patient is myopic within the neutral point
fields.option_c: The patient is hypermetropic or undercorrected
fields.option_d: The patient is at the neutral point
fields.option_e: The retinoscope is too close to the eye
fields.explanation: ‘With’ movement of the reflex indicates that the patient is hypermetropic or undercorrected — the far point lies behind the observer, requiring additional plus power to reach neutrality.

fields.text: A plano-convex lens has its principal focal point located:
fields.explanation: In a plano-convex lens, the principal focus lies on the convex side where parallel rays converge after refraction. The plane side allows light to enter with minimal deviation.

fields.text: Which of the following may be accompanied by a falsely low Goldmann IOP measurement?
fields.explanation: Goldmann applanation tonometry underestimates IOP in eyes with thinner or biomechanically weaker corneas, such as after laser refractive surgery or in corneal oedema. It may also read falsely low with too little fluorescein dye or in high myopia.

fields.text: Goldmann applanation tonometry is based on which physical principle?
fields.explanation: Goldmann tonometry is based on the Imbert–Fick principle, which assumes that the pressure inside a thin, dry, perfectly elastic sphere equals the external force divided by the flattened area. The cornea only approximates these conditions, leading to sources of error.

fields.text: Which of the following will cause Goldmann applanation tonometry readings to be falsely high?
fields.explanation: A thicker or more rigid cornea requires greater applanation force, leading to falsely elevated IOP readings. This can occur with increased corneal thickness or with applanation over corneal scars.

fields.text: Which factor may lead to falsely low IOP readings on Goldmann tonometry?
fields.explanation: Corneal oedema softens the corneal surface, reducing resistance to applanation and causing falsely low readings. Thin corneas and post-LASIK corneas have similar effects.

fields.text: Which of the following statements about Goldmann applanation tonometry is TRUE?
fields.option_a: The tonometer assumes a corneal diameter of 12 mm
fields.option_b: The applanated area is 7.3 mm in diameter
fields.option_c: A fluorescein ring too wide indicates excessive dye or pressure
fields.option_d: It measures IOP by indentation of the cornea
fields.option_e: The cornea is assumed to be rigid and impermeable
fields.explanation: A wide fluorescein ring during Goldmann tonometry indicates either excessive fluorescein or over-applanation. The optimal ring width is about 0.3 mm.

fields.text: In a patient with keratoconus, Goldmann applanation tonometry readings are typically:
fields.option_a: Falsely high due to irregular astigmatism
fields.option_b: Falsely low due to reduced corneal rigidity
fields.option_c: Unaffected as the corneal apex is central
fields.option_d: Accurate when performed over the cone apex
fields.explanation: In keratoconus, the cornea is thinner and biomechanically weaker, leading to underestimation of IOP by Goldmann tonometry. Alternative tonometers such as dynamic contour tonometers may provide more accurate results.

fields.text: Tonometry readings in a patient with high myopia are likely to be:
fields.option_a: Falsely high due to scleral stiffness
fields.option_b: Falsely low due to decreased scleral rigidity
fields.option_c: Accurate when measured at the corneal apex
fields.explanation: High myopia is associated with reduced scleral rigidity, which can lead to underestimation of IOP on applanation tonometry.

fields.text: In Goldmann applanation tonometry, which parameter has the greatest impact on accuracy?
fields.explanation: Corneal thickness and biomechanical rigidity most significantly affect Goldmann tonometry accuracy. A thicker or stiffer cornea leads to falsely high readings, while thinner or weaker corneas give falsely low readings.

fields.text: Which of the following tonometers is least affected by corneal thickness?
fields.explanation: The dynamic contour tonometer (Pascal) measures IOP by contour matching rather than flattening the cornea. It is less influenced by corneal thickness and biomechanics compared to Goldmann tonometry.

fields.text: Which of the following fluorescein patterns indicates correct applanation during Goldmann tonometry?
fields.option_a: Two thick green semicircles touching at the inner edges
fields.option_b: Two thin yellow arcs overlapping centrally
fields.option_c: Two equal semicircular rings just touching at their inner margins
fields.option_e: Unequal ring thickness on both sides
fields.explanation: Correct applanation is achieved when two equal, thin fluorescein semicircles just touch at their inner margins. Thick or uneven rings indicate excess fluorescein or incorrect alignment.

fields.text: In a light-adapted patient, a blue flash of light will generate an electroretinogram (ERG) with:
fields.option_a: A combination of rod and cone input
fields.explanation: Even under light-adapted conditions, some rod activity remains. A blue flash stimulates both rods and short-wavelength-sensitive cones. Pure cone isolation requires flicker stimuli or long-wavelength (red/orange) light.

fields.text: In a dark-adapted electroretinogram (ERG), which component represents the activity of the photoreceptors?
fields.explanation: The a-wave is the initial negative deflection in the ERG and represents photoreceptor hyperpolarization, mainly from rods under dark-adapted conditions.

fields.text: The b-wave in a full-field electroretinogram primarily reflects activity from:
fields.explanation: The b-wave is generated mainly by depolarizing bipolar cells with contributions from Müller cells. It represents post-photoreceptor retinal activity.

fields.text: Which ERG test condition isolates cone function most effectively?
fields.explanation: A light-adapted 30 Hz flicker ERG isolates cone function since rods cannot respond at such high temporal frequencies.

fields.text: A flat ERG with a normal electro-oculogram (EOG) suggests:
fields.explanation: Leber congenital amaurosis causes a flat or absent ERG due to widespread photoreceptor dysfunction, but the EOG may initially remain normal.

fields.text: A patient with Best vitelliform macular dystrophy would typically show which of the following findings?
fields.option_b: Normal ERG but reduced EOG Arden ratio
fields.explanation: In Best disease, the ERG is normal (general retinal function intact) but the EOG shows a reduced light peak-to-dark trough ratio (Arden ratio <1.5).

fields.text: The Arden ratio in the electro-oculogram (EOG) is defined as:
fields.option_a: Amplitude of the a-wave divided by the b-wave
fields.option_b: Light peak amplitude divided by dark trough amplitude
fields.option_e: Ratio of macular to peripheral response
fields.explanation: The Arden ratio is the ratio of the light peak to the dark trough in the EOG, assessing retinal pigment epithelium function. Normal values are greater than 1.8.

fields.text: Which of the following findings is typical in retinitis pigmentosa on ERG testing?
fields.option_b: Absent rod response with reduced cone response
fields.option_d: Normal ERG with delayed implicit time
fields.explanation: Retinitis pigmentosa primarily affects rods first, leading to absent rod responses and reduced cone function as the disease progresses.

fields.text: Which visual pathway abnormality produces a normal ERG but abnormal visual evoked potential (VEP)?
fields.explanation: A normal ERG with an abnormal VEP indicates post-retinal (optic nerve or visual pathway) dysfunction, as in optic neuritis or compressive optic neuropathy.

fields.text: A delayed P100 latency on pattern visual evoked potential (VEP) is most suggestive of:
fields.explanation: A delayed P100 latency with preserved amplitude is characteristic of demyelinating optic neuropathy (optic neuritis). The P100 represents cortical response latency to visual stimuli.

fields.text: Which children's acuity test uses crowded letters?
fields.explanation: The Sonksen–Silver test uses crowded letters to simulate the difficulty of reading lines of letters, improving detection of amblyopia. Sheridan–Gardiner uses single optotypes. Kay pictures use matching, Keeler is a preferential looking test, and Cardiff cards present white-on-grey pictures.

fields.text: Which of the following tests is most suitable for assessing vision in a 12-month-old infant?
fields.explanation: Cardiff cards are preferential looking tests suitable for infants around 6–24 months. They rely on the child’s tendency to look at patterned stimuli rather than blank areas.

fields.text: Which visual acuity test is based on preferential looking?
fields.explanation: The Keeler preferential looking test uses gratings of different spatial frequencies to estimate visual acuity by observing fixation preference. It is used in preverbal children.

fields.text: The Kay picture test primarily assesses acuity using:
fields.explanation: The Kay picture test uses picture-matching optotypes, making it ideal for children who cannot recognize letters but can identify or match familiar objects.

fields.text: Which test uses white pictures on a grey background for young children?
fields.explanation: Cardiff cards present simple white pictures (e.g. house, duck) on a grey background, exploiting infants’ fixation preference to determine acuity.

fields.text: Which test is most appropriate for a 3-year-old child with amblyopia under treatment?
fields.explanation: For children aged 2.5–5 years, the crowded Kay picture test provides accurate assessment of amblyopia due to its crowding phenomenon similar to letter charts.

fields.text: Crowding in visual acuity testing refers to:
fields.option_b: Grouping of optotypes that makes recognition harder
fields.explanation: Crowding describes the reduced ability to recognize a target when it is surrounded by other symbols or letters. It is particularly important for detecting amblyopia.

fields.text: The Sheridan–Gardiner test is most accurately described as:
fields.option_b: A letter matching test using single optotypes
fields.explanation: The Sheridan–Gardiner test uses single letters for recognition and matching, making it suitable for preschool children who can match but not yet read letters.

fields.text: Which visual acuity test can detect crowding effects similar to the adult LogMAR chart?
fields.explanation: Crowded LogMAR charts replicate adult testing by including linear arrangements of optotypes with controlled spacing, allowing detection of amblyopia and comparison across ages.

fields.text: Which of the following pediatric tests provides the best assessment of visual acuity under standard clinical lighting conditions?
fields.explanation: The crowded LogMAR test provides the most standardized and accurate measure of visual acuity in older children, using controlled spacing and contrast similar to adult charts.

fields.text: Which of the following is a test for eccentric fixation?
fields.explanation: Visioscopy assesses eccentric fixation using a direct ophthalmoscope with a reticule target. The patient fixates centrally, and the examiner measures the deviation between the fixation point and the fovea on the reticule scale.

fields.text: Eccentric fixation is most commonly associated with:
fields.explanation: Eccentric fixation occurs when a patient habitually uses a non-foveal point for fixation, most commonly in microtropia or dense amblyopia, leading to reduced visual acuity.

fields.text: The Worth 4-dot test primarily assesses:
fields.explanation: The Worth 4-dot test assesses sensory fusion and suppression. The patient views a four-dot pattern through red-green filters; the number and color of dots seen indicate suppression, diplopia, or fusion.

fields.text: Bagolini striated lenses are used to evaluate:
fields.explanation: Bagolini striated glasses test for suppression and abnormal retinal correspondence (ARC) under natural binocular viewing. Each lens produces a streak; the pattern of lines perceived reveals the sensory status.

fields.text: The Titmus fly test assesses:
fields.explanation: The Titmus fly test is a near stereopsis test based on polarized images. The presence of depth perception when viewing the 3D fly image indicates functioning binocular disparity detection.

fields.text: Which of the following tests measures sensory fusion under natural viewing conditions?
fields.explanation: The Bagolini test allows binocular fusion assessment under near-natural viewing. Unlike Worth 4-dot or synoptophore, it causes minimal dissociation, providing a realistic evaluation of binocular sensory status.

fields.text: Abnormal retinal correspondence (ARC) most commonly develops as an adaptation to:
fields.explanation: ARC is an adaptive mechanism in constant unilateral strabismus where the fovea of the fixing eye corresponds to an extrafoveal area in the deviating eye, preventing diplopia.

fields.text: Which test is most suitable for measuring motor fusion amplitude?
fields.explanation: The synoptophore (major amblyoscope) quantifies motor fusion by altering vergence angles while maintaining fusion. It measures the range of fusional convergence and divergence.

fields.text: Which test is most appropriate to identify suppression scotoma in a child with microtropia?
fields.explanation: The Worth 4-dot test helps identify suppression. In microtropia, the patient may suppress one image centrally but maintain peripheral fusion, revealing a small central suppression scotoma.

fields.text: Which clinical finding best differentiates anomalous retinal correspondence from eccentric fixation?
fields.option_c: Angle of anomaly measured on synoptophore
fields.explanation: ARC is demonstrated using a synoptophore, where the subjective and objective angles differ. Eccentric fixation is measured directly on visioscopy and does not involve retinal correspondence changes.

fields.text: All of the following are mandatory in monitoring a patient with thyroid eye disease EXCEPT:
fields.explanation: Fluorescein angiography is not routinely indicated in thyroid eye disease (TED). Monitoring focuses on optic nerve function through visual acuity, fields, color vision, and RAPD, as well as intraocular pressure in primary and upgaze.

fields.text: Which test best detects early optic nerve compression in thyroid eye disease?
fields.explanation: Early compressive optic neuropathy in thyroid eye disease often presents with reduced color vision before loss of visual acuity or field changes, making it an early indicator of optic nerve compromise.

fields.text: Which investigation is most helpful for confirming the diagnosis of thyroid eye disease?
fields.explanation: CT of the orbits demonstrates extraocular muscle enlargement with tendon sparing, characteristic of thyroid eye disease. It also helps assess optic nerve compression and orbital apex crowding.

fields.text: In thyroid eye disease, raised intraocular pressure in upgaze is most commonly due to:
fields.option_b: Restriction of the inferior rectus muscle
fields.explanation: Restriction of the inferior rectus in thyroid eye disease limits upgaze and increases intraocular pressure due to mechanical compression of the globe during attempted elevation.

fields.text: In a patient with suspected dysthyroid optic neuropathy, which finding is most alarming?
fields.explanation: Reduced color vision (especially red desaturation) is an early and sensitive sign of optic nerve compression in dysthyroid optic neuropathy, warranting urgent management.

fields.text: In thyroid eye disease, which extraocular muscle is most commonly involved?
fields.explanation: The inferior rectus is most commonly affected in thyroid eye disease, followed by the medial rectus, superior rectus, and lateral rectus (mnemonic: 'IM SLo').

fields.text: In the context of thyroid eye disease, which of the following features on imaging suggests optic nerve compression?
fields.option_a: Apical crowding by enlarged extraocular muscles
fields.explanation: Optic nerve compression occurs due to apical crowding by enlarged extraocular muscles within the confined bony orbit. CT imaging demonstrates this clearly.

fields.text: In thyroid eye disease, which clinical test best assesses restrictive myopathy?
fields.explanation: The forced duction test determines whether ocular motility limitation is due to mechanical restriction (positive test) or neurogenic cause (negative test). It is key in thyroid myopathy evaluation.

fields.text: Which scoring system is used to assess activity in thyroid eye disease?
fields.explanation: The Clinical Activity Score (CAS) evaluates inflammatory activity in thyroid eye disease. A score ≥3/7 suggests active disease requiring immunosuppressive treatment.

fields.text: Which grading system provides a descriptive classification of thyroid eye disease severity?
fields.explanation: The NOSPECS classification categorizes thyroid eye disease severity into stages based on tissue involvement: No signs, Only signs, Soft tissue, Proptosis, Extraocular muscle, Corneal, and Sight loss.

fields.text: Which is FALSE regarding sterilization:
fields.option_a: it involves the destruction of all microbial life so that none can replicate
fields.option_b: it can be achieved with ethylene oxide
fields.option_c: dry heat is more effective than wet heat
fields.option_d: it can be achieved at high pressures using an autoclave
fields.option_e: it can be achieved with gamma radiation
fields.explanation: Moist heat is more effective than dry heat, which requires higher temperatures and longer exposure to achieve sterilization.

fields.text: Autoclave sterilization typically uses which of the following combinations of temperature and pressure?
fields.explanation: The standard autoclave cycle for sterilization uses moist heat at 121°C under 15 psi pressure for 15–20 minutes.

fields.text: Ethylene oxide sterilization is most suitable for:
fields.option_b: Heat-sensitive materials like plastic syringes and catheters
fields.explanation: Ethylene oxide gas is used for sterilizing heat- and moisture-sensitive items such as plastics, rubber, and electronic devices.

fields.text: Which of the following methods achieves sterilization through ionizing radiation?
fields.explanation: Gamma radiation is ionizing and penetrates deeply, destroying DNA and effectively sterilizing disposable medical items and pharmaceuticals.

fields.text: Which of the following statements about dry heat sterilization is TRUE?
fields.option_a: It is faster and more efficient than moist heat
fields.option_b: It destroys microorganisms by protein coagulation
fields.option_c: It sterilizes by oxidation of cell components
fields.option_e: It is performed at 100°C for 15 minutes
fields.explanation: Dry heat sterilization kills microorganisms through oxidative destruction of cellular components and is used for glassware and metal instruments.

fields.text: Which of the following items cannot be sterilized by an autoclave?
fields.explanation: Powders and oils cannot be sterilized by moist heat as they do not allow steam penetration. Dry heat sterilization is used instead.

fields.text: Filtration sterilization is most appropriate for:
fields.explanation: Filtration is used for sterilizing heat-labile fluids like antibiotics or serum that would be denatured by heat.

fields.text: Which of the following accurately differentiates disinfection from sterilization?
fields.option_a: Disinfection destroys all forms of microbial life including spores
fields.option_c: Disinfection reduces microbial count but may not remove spores
fields.explanation: Disinfection destroys vegetative forms of microorganisms but may not kill spores, whereas sterilization eliminates all microbial life including spores.

fields.text: Pasteurization is best described as:
fields.option_a: A sterilization technique for surgical instruments
fields.option_b: A disinfection process using moist heat below 100°C
fields.option_c: A dry heat method for sterilizing powders
fields.explanation: Pasteurization uses moist heat at temperatures below 100°C to reduce microbial load in liquids such as milk, but does not achieve sterilization.

fields.text: Which combination correctly matches the sterilization method with its mechanism?
fields.explanation: Moist heat causes coagulation and denaturation of proteins, effectively killing microorganisms including spores.

fields.text: Which layer of the cornea is primarily affected in disciform keratitis caused by HSV?
fields.explanation: Disciform keratitis affects the stroma and endothelium due to an immune-mediated reaction to viral antigens rather than direct viral invasion.

fields.text: Which statement about HSV epithelial keratitis is FALSE?
fields.option_a: It typically presents with branching dendritic ulcers with terminal bulbs
fields.option_b: It stains with fluorescein centrally and rose bengal at the edges
fields.option_c: Topical corticosteroids are the mainstay of treatment
fields.option_d: It can result in reduced corneal sensation
fields.option_e: Recurrent infection may cause scarring and irregular astigmatism
fields.explanation: Topical corticosteroids are contraindicated in active epithelial HSV infection as they worsen viral replication. Antivirals such as acyclovir are used instead.

fields.text: Which of the following is TRUE regarding HSV stromal keratitis?
fields.option_a: It is caused by active viral replication within the corneal stroma
fields.option_b: It represents an immune reaction to viral antigens
fields.option_c: It is best treated with topical antiviral monotherapy
fields.option_d: It has no association with endothelial dysfunction
fields.option_e: It is associated with conjunctival scarring
fields.explanation: HSV stromal keratitis results from a delayed-type hypersensitivity reaction to viral antigens, leading to stromal inflammation and scarring.

fields.text: Which statement regarding HSV and VZV is TRUE?
fields.option_b: Both establish latency in the trigeminal ganglion
fields.option_c: HSV remains latent in the dorsal root ganglia only
fields.option_d: VZV does not cause corneal disease
fields.option_e: HSV-1 is primarily transmitted by respiratory droplets
fields.explanation: Both HSV-1 and varicella-zoster virus (VZV) establish latency in sensory ganglia, including the trigeminal ganglion, and can reactivate to cause ocular disease.

fields.text: Which of the following findings is most characteristic of HSV epithelial keratitis?
fields.option_a: Linear epithelial defect with no terminal bulbs
fields.option_c: Circular stromal infiltration with overlying intact epithelium
fields.explanation: The presence of a branching dendritic ulcer with terminal bulbs is diagnostic of HSV epithelial keratitis.

fields.text: Which type of immune reaction underlies HSV stromal keratitis?
fields.explanation: HSV stromal keratitis is due to a Type IV (delayed-type) hypersensitivity reaction against viral antigens within the cornea.

fields.text: What is the most appropriate treatment for HSV disciform keratitis?
fields.option_b: Topical corticosteroids with concurrent antiviral cover
fields.explanation: HSV disciform keratitis is immune-mediated and treated with topical steroids to control inflammation, always with concurrent antiviral therapy to prevent viral reactivation.

fields.text: Recurrent HSV keratitis may lead to which long-term complication?
fields.explanation: Recurrent HSV keratitis damages corneal nerves, resulting in reduced sensation and neurotrophic keratopathy.

fields.text: Which laboratory technique is most sensitive for confirming HSV infection?
fields.explanation: PCR is the most sensitive and specific diagnostic test for HSV infection, allowing detection of viral DNA even at low levels.

fields.text: Which antiviral agent is used for systemic suppression of recurrent HSV keratitis?
fields.explanation: Oral valacyclovir or acyclovir is used for long-term prophylaxis to reduce recurrence of HSV keratitis.

fields.text: All are true of Acanthamoeba EXCEPT:
fields.option_a: it can be cultured from the nasopharynx of healthy individuals
fields.option_b: corneal neovascularisation is not a typical early finding
fields.option_c: it is the most common cause of infective keratitis in contact lens wearers
fields.option_d: it can be killed with 3% hydrogen peroxide
fields.explanation: Pseudomonas aeruginosa, not Acanthamoeba, is the most common cause of contact lens–associated keratitis. Acanthamoeba is an uncommon but serious infection.

fields.text: Which of the following statements regarding Acanthamoeba keratitis is TRUE?
fields.option_a: It is caused by a gram-negative bacterium
fields.option_b: It typically causes pain out of proportion to clinical signs
fields.option_c: It is usually responsive to standard antibacterial therapy
fields.option_d: It is confined to the corneal epithelium
fields.option_e: It is transmitted by human-to-human contact
fields.explanation: Acanthamoeba keratitis characteristically causes severe pain that appears disproportionate to slit-lamp findings. It is caused by a free-living protozoan found in water and soil.

fields.text: Which of the following is the most important risk factor for Acanthamoeba keratitis?
fields.option_b: Swimming or showering while wearing contact lenses
fields.explanation: Acanthamoeba thrives in water, and exposure during swimming, showering, or using non-sterile saline with lenses increases infection risk.

fields.text: Which clinical feature is LEAST likely to be seen in early Acanthamoeba keratitis?
fields.explanation: Corneal neovascularisation is an uncommon and late feature. Early signs include punctate epithelial changes, perineural infiltrates, and severe pain.

fields.text: Which of the following best describes Acanthamoeba?
fields.explanation: Acanthamoeba is a free-living protozoan found in soil, tap water, and swimming pools that can infect the cornea, particularly in contact lens wearers.

fields.text: The classic ring infiltrate seen in Acanthamoeba keratitis represents:
fields.option_c: An immune response to trophozoites and cysts in the cornea
fields.explanation: The ring-shaped stromal infiltrate reflects an immune response to both the trophozoite and cystic stages of Acanthamoeba in the corneal stroma.

fields.text: Which statement about the lifecycle of Acanthamoeba is TRUE?
fields.option_a: Only the trophozoite form is resistant to disinfection
fields.option_b: The cyst form is highly resistant to treatment and adverse conditions
fields.option_d: Cysts form only inside host tissue
fields.option_e: Trophozoites cannot survive outside a host
fields.explanation: Acanthamoeba exists as trophozoites and cysts; the cyst form is highly resistant to treatment and harsh environmental conditions.

fields.text: Which of the following disinfectants is most effective against Acanthamoeba cysts?
fields.explanation: Polyhexamethylene biguanide (PHMB) and chlorhexidine in combination are effective against both cyst and trophozoite forms of Acanthamoeba.

fields.text: Which of the following differentiates Acanthamoeba keratitis from bacterial keratitis?
fields.option_c: Greenish corneal infiltrate with mucopurulent discharge
fields.explanation: Pain that is disproportionate to slit-lamp signs is a key distinguishing feature of Acanthamoeba keratitis compared to bacterial causes.

fields.text: Which organism is the most common cause of contact lens–associated microbial keratitis?
fields.explanation: Pseudomonas aeruginosa is the most frequent cause of microbial keratitis in contact lens wearers, producing rapid corneal melting and ulceration.

fields.text: What is the most basic difference between Candida and Fusarium?
fields.option_a: Fusarium is dimorphic, and Candida is a mold
fields.option_b: Fusarium is a mold, and Candida is dimorphic
fields.option_c: Fusarium as a yeast has pseudohyphae, whereas Candida has true hyphae
fields.option_d: Fusarium is a yeast, and Candida is a mold
fields.option_e: Fusarium is a mold, and Candida is a yeast
fields.explanation: Fusarium is a filamentous mold, whereas Candida is a dimorphic fungus that exists as a yeast and can form pseudohyphae. Fungi are grouped as molds, yeasts, or dimorphs depending on morphology and growth conditions.

fields.text: Which of the following fungi is most commonly isolated in cases of fungal keratitis in tropical climates?
fields.explanation: Fusarium is the most common cause of fungal keratitis in tropical and subtropical regions, especially following trauma involving plant material.

fields.text: Which of the following best describes Candida keratitis?
fields.option_a: Usually follows trauma with vegetative matter
fields.option_b: Typically affects compromised corneas or those on chronic corticosteroids
fields.option_c: Presents with dry raised feathery margins
fields.option_d: Commonly causes ring infiltrates and satellite lesions
fields.explanation: Candida keratitis is an opportunistic infection that occurs in compromised corneas, such as those with chronic ocular surface disease, previous surgery, or long-term corticosteroid use.

fields.text: Which of the following clinical features is most characteristic of Fusarium keratitis?
fields.option_a: Gray-white dry ulcer with feathery edges and satellite lesions
fields.option_b: Creamy yellow infiltrate with smooth margins
fields.option_c: Ring-shaped stromal infiltrate with perineural spread
fields.explanation: Fusarium keratitis classically presents with a dry gray-white corneal ulcer that has feathery margins and satellite lesions, typically following trauma with vegetative material.

fields.text: Which of the following is the preferred initial antifungal therapy for filamentous fungal keratitis?
fields.explanation: Natamycin 5% is the first-line topical antifungal for filamentous fungi such as Fusarium and Aspergillus. Amphotericin B and azoles are more effective for yeast infections like Candida.

fields.text: Which fungal organism is most likely to cause keratitis following trauma with a vegetative object?
fields.explanation: Fusarium species are the most common cause of post-traumatic fungal keratitis, particularly when organic or vegetative matter is involved.

fields.text: Which feature differentiates fungal keratitis from bacterial keratitis?
fields.option_b: Pain out of proportion to signs
fields.explanation: Fungal ulcers often have dry, feathery edges with satellite lesions, distinguishing them from bacterial ulcers that show dense suppuration and rapid progression.

fields.text: Which stain is most useful for demonstrating fungal filaments in corneal scrapings?
fields.explanation: Calcofluor white binds to chitin in fungal cell walls and fluoresces under UV light, making it highly effective for detecting fungal filaments in corneal specimens.

fields.text: Which statement regarding Aspergillus keratitis is TRUE?
fields.option_a: It typically presents with a creamy, smooth ulcer surface
fields.option_b: It is more common in immunocompromised hosts and after trauma with organic matter
fields.option_c: It is a yeast-like infection confined to the corneal epithelium
fields.option_d: It rarely leads to hypopyon formation
fields.option_e: It responds rapidly to antibacterial therapy
fields.explanation: Aspergillus keratitis commonly affects individuals following corneal trauma with plant material and may lead to deep stromal infection with hypopyon formation.

fields.text: Which of the following antifungal agents achieves good intraocular penetration and can be used systemically?
fields.explanation: Fluconazole has excellent ocular and intraocular penetration and is effective systemically against Candida species. It is less effective against filamentous fungi like Fusarium.

fields.text: All are true of E.coli EXCEPT:
fields.option_c: it grows well on MacConkey agar
fields.option_d: it is a Gram negative rod
fields.explanation: E. coli is motile with peritrichous flagella, not non-motile. It is a Gram-negative, facultative anaerobic rod that ferments lactose and grows well on MacConkey agar.

fields.text: Which of the following best describes Pseudomonas aeruginosa?
fields.option_b: Gram-negative rod with a fruity odour and blue-green pigment
fields.explanation: Pseudomonas aeruginosa is a motile Gram-negative rod producing blue-green pigments (pyocyanin and pyoverdin) and a characteristic fruity odour.

fields.text: Which of the following is TRUE about Pseudomonas keratitis?
fields.option_a: It is commonly associated with vegetative trauma
fields.option_b: It is the most frequent cause of bacterial keratitis in contact lens wearers
fields.option_d: It is typically indolent and slow to progress
fields.option_e: It is sensitive to penicillin G
fields.explanation: Pseudomonas aeruginosa is the leading cause of bacterial keratitis in contact-lens wearers and may cause rapid corneal melting with hypopyon formation.

fields.text: Which feature distinguishes Pseudomonas from Enterobacteriaceae family members such as E. coli?
fields.option_c: Ability to grow on MacConkey agar
fields.explanation: Pseudomonas is oxidase-positive, unlike Enterobacteriaceae (e.g., E. coli), which are oxidase-negative. Both are Gram-negative rods capable of motility and endotoxin production.

fields.text: Which of the following is TRUE regarding Neisseria gonorrhoeae?
fields.option_c: It can penetrate intact corneal epithelium
fields.explanation: Neisseria gonorrhoeae is a Gram-negative diplococcus capable of penetrating intact corneal epithelium, leading to rapidly progressive hyperacute keratoconjunctivitis.

fields.text: Which agar is selective for the growth of Neisseria species?
fields.explanation: Thayer-Martin medium (chocolate agar containing vancomycin, nystatin, and colistin) is selective for Neisseria species such as N. gonorrhoeae and N. meningitidis.

fields.text: Which of the following ocular conditions is most commonly caused by Moraxella species?
fields.explanation: Moraxella lacunata, a Gram-negative diplobacillus, is associated with angular blepharoconjunctivitis, especially in patients with poor hygiene or alcoholism.

fields.text: Which of the following statements about Haemophilus influenzae is TRUE?
fields.option_b: It requires X and V factors for growth
fields.option_c: It grows readily on nutrient agar without supplements
fields.option_d: It causes only systemic infection, not ocular
fields.explanation: Haemophilus influenzae is a small Gram-negative coccobacillus requiring both X (hemin) and V (NAD) factors for growth. It can cause conjunctivitis, especially in children.

fields.text: Which Gram-negative organism is most often implicated in endophthalmitis following penetrating ocular trauma?
fields.explanation: Pseudomonas aeruginosa is a frequent cause of post-traumatic endophthalmitis due to its aggressive tissue invasion and rapid progression.

fields.text: Which of the following statements regarding Klebsiella pneumoniae is TRUE?
fields.option_b: It is motile with peritrichous flagella
fields.option_c: It produces a prominent capsule and causes endogenous endophthalmitis
fields.option_d: It does not grow on MacConkey agar
fields.explanation: Klebsiella pneumoniae is a non-motile, encapsulated Gram-negative rod that can cause endogenous endophthalmitis, particularly in diabetic and septic patients.

fields.text: Foetal abnormalities can occur if a mother becomes infected with all of the following EXCEPT:
fields.explanation: Hepatitis A does not cause congenital infection or foetal malformations. The classic TORCH infections that cause foetal abnormalities are Toxoplasmosis, Other (Syphilis, Listeria, HIV), Rubella, Cytomegalovirus, and Herpes.

fields.text: Which of the following is NOT a typical feature of congenital toxoplasmosis?
fields.explanation: The classic triad of congenital toxoplasmosis includes chorioretinitis, hydrocephalus, and intracranial calcification. Blue sclera is seen in osteogenesis imperfecta, not congenital infection.

fields.text: Which of the following TORCH infections most commonly causes salt-and-pepper retinopathy?
fields.explanation: Congenital rubella classically causes salt-and-pepper retinopathy along with cataract, microphthalmia, and sensorineural deafness.

fields.text: Which congenital infection is most strongly associated with chorioretinitis and periventricular calcifications?
fields.explanation: Congenital CMV infection causes periventricular calcifications and chorioretinitis. Toxoplasmosis also causes chorioretinitis but with diffuse or basal ganglia calcifications.

fields.text: Which of the following statements about congenital rubella syndrome is TRUE?
fields.option_a: It occurs if infection occurs after 24 weeks of gestation
fields.option_b: It is associated with microcephaly, cataract, and sensorineural deafness
fields.option_d: It is caused by a DNA virus
fields.option_e: It is prevented by passive immunization at birth
fields.explanation: Congenital rubella syndrome is caused by maternal infection during the first trimester and results in cataract, microcephaly, and sensorineural hearing loss. Rubella is an RNA virus prevented by vaccination.

fields.text: Which of the following features is characteristic of congenital syphilis?
fields.explanation: Interstitial keratitis, Hutchinson’s teeth, and sensorineural deafness make up Hutchinson’s triad — the hallmark of late congenital syphilis.

fields.text: Congenital herpes simplex infection most often presents with:
fields.explanation: Congenital HSV infection typically manifests with encephalitis, chorioretinitis, and vesicular skin lesions. It can lead to severe neurological and ocular morbidity.

fields.text: Which maternal infection is most likely to cause spontaneous abortion or stillbirth rather than congenital malformation?
fields.explanation: Listeria monocytogenes infection in pregnancy often leads to spontaneous abortion or stillbirth rather than congenital structural defects.

fields.text: Which of the following congenital infections is most likely to cause cataract and microphthalmia?
fields.explanation: Congenital rubella causes cataract, microphthalmia, and pigmentary retinopathy — key ocular features of rubella embryopathy.

fields.text: Which maternal infection can cause both microcephaly and chorioretinitis in the neonate?
fields.explanation: Cytomegalovirus is a leading cause of congenital microcephaly and chorioretinitis. It produces periventricular calcifications and sensory deficits.

fields.text: Which is FALSE regarding adenovirus:
fields.option_a: can be classified into more than 40 serotypes
fields.option_d: causes keratitis which is unresponsive to acyclovir but may respond to topical steroid
fields.explanation: Adenovirus is a non-enveloped, double-stranded DNA virus. It causes epidemic keratoconjunctivitis, which is unresponsive to acyclovir as it is not a herpes virus.

fields.text: Which adenovirus serotypes are most commonly associated with epidemic keratoconjunctivitis (EKC)?
fields.explanation: Adenovirus serotypes 8, 19, and 37 are classically associated with epidemic keratoconjunctivitis (EKC), a highly contagious ocular infection.

fields.text: Which clinical feature is characteristic of adenoviral keratoconjunctivitis?
fields.explanation: Adenoviral keratoconjunctivitis may produce subepithelial infiltrates weeks after acute infection due to immune response against viral antigens in the cornea.

fields.text: Which of the following statements about Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV) keratitis is TRUE?
fields.option_b: Dendritic ulcers stain positively with rose bengal at their base
fields.option_c: Topical steroids should be started immediately
fields.option_d: It is caused by HSV-2 in most cases
fields.option_e: Disciform keratitis is due to active viral replication
fields.explanation: HSV dendritic ulcers stain brightly with fluorescein at the edges and rose bengal at the base due to damaged epithelial cells containing virus.

fields.text: Disciform keratitis is best described as:
fields.option_a: Necrotizing infection of the corneal stroma with ulceration
fields.option_b: Immune reaction to viral antigens within the corneal stroma
fields.option_c: Superficial bacterial co-infection after viral epithelial keratitis
fields.option_d: Involvement of the endothelium by adenovirus
fields.option_e: Fungal infection of the posterior cornea
fields.explanation: Disciform keratitis is an immune-mediated reaction to viral antigens within the stroma or endothelium, often seen in herpes simplex or zoster infections.

fields.text: Which of the following best describes herpes zoster ophthalmicus?
fields.option_a: Infection of the trigeminal ophthalmic branch by varicella-zoster virus
fields.option_b: Reactivation of HSV in the maxillary branch of the trigeminal nerve
fields.option_d: Viral infection of the lacrimal gland only
fields.explanation: Herpes zoster ophthalmicus is caused by reactivation of latent varicella-zoster virus in the ophthalmic division of the trigeminal nerve (V1).

fields.text: Hutchinson’s sign in herpes zoster ophthalmicus refers to:
fields.option_b: Vesicular eruption at the tip of the nose indicating nasociliary involvement
fields.explanation: Hutchinson’s sign indicates vesicles on the tip of the nose due to involvement of the nasociliary branch, predicting ocular complications in herpes zoster ophthalmicus.

fields.text: Which of the following viruses causes umbilicated lesions on the eyelid margin that shed infectious material into the conjunctival sac?
fields.explanation: Molluscum contagiosum causes umbilicated eyelid lesions that can shed viral particles into the conjunctival sac, causing chronic follicular conjunctivitis.

fields.text: Which antiviral is most effective for the treatment of varicella-zoster ophthalmicus?
fields.explanation: Oral acyclovir is the first-line treatment for varicella-zoster ophthalmicus, especially when started within 72 hours of rash onset.

fields.text: Which of the following best differentiates herpes simplex keratitis from herpes zoster ophthalmicus?
fields.option_a: Presence of pseudodendritic corneal lesions in zoster
fields.option_b: Sensitivity to acyclovir in zoster only
fields.option_c: Involvement of V2 dermatome in HSV
fields.option_d: Disciform keratitis in HSV but not in zoster
fields.option_e: Lack of corneal sensation in HSV only
fields.explanation: Herpes zoster ophthalmicus produces pseudodendritic lesions lacking terminal bulbs, unlike the true dendrites of HSV keratitis.

fields.text: Normal flora of the conjunctiva include all EXCEPT:
fields.explanation: Nocardia are not part of the normal conjunctival flora. The common commensals include coagulase-negative staphylococci (S. epidermidis), S. aureus, and S. viridans.

fields.text: Which of the following bacteria is most commonly associated with blepharitis and chronic conjunctivitis?
fields.explanation: Staphylococcus aureus is the most frequent cause of chronic blepharitis and recurrent conjunctivitis due to its ability to colonize eyelid margins and produce exotoxins.

fields.text: Which statement about Staphylococcus aureus is TRUE?
fields.option_d: It is non-pathogenic on the ocular surface
fields.option_e: It is the most common cause of fungal keratitis
fields.explanation: S. aureus is a Gram-positive, catalase- and coagulase-positive coccus that commonly causes blepharitis, conjunctivitis, and bacterial keratitis.

fields.text: Which of the following bacteria is most frequently associated with post-traumatic keratitis involving vegetative matter?
fields.explanation: Bacillus cereus is a spore-forming Gram-positive rod that causes rapidly progressive keratitis and endophthalmitis after ocular trauma with vegetative material.

fields.text: Which of the following organisms can penetrate an intact corneal epithelium?
fields.explanation: All of these organisms — S. pneumoniae, P. aeruginosa, N. gonorrhoeae, and H. influenzae — have the capability to penetrate an intact corneal epithelium under certain conditions.

fields.text: Which of the following bacteria is a Gram-positive, filamentous, weakly acid-fast organism that can cause keratitis?
fields.explanation: Nocardia asteroides is a Gram-positive, filamentous, weakly acid-fast bacterium found in soil and associated with keratitis, particularly after trauma.

fields.text: Which of the following bacteria is most commonly implicated in bacterial corneal ulcers following trauma with a fingernail?
fields.explanation: Streptococcus pneumoniae (pneumococcus) is a major cause of corneal ulceration following fingernail trauma or in eyes with pre-existing corneal disease.

fields.text: Which statement about Corynebacterium diphtheriae ocular infection is TRUE?
fields.option_c: It is part of the normal ocular flora
fields.explanation: Corynebacterium diphtheriae causes pseudomembranous conjunctivitis due to exotoxin-mediated epithelial necrosis and fibrinous exudation.

fields.text: Which of the following is the most common Gram-positive organism isolated from postoperative endophthalmitis?
fields.explanation: Staphylococcus epidermidis, part of the normal conjunctival flora, is the most common cause of postoperative endophthalmitis due to its ability to form biofilms on intraocular lenses.

fields.text: Which of the following statements about Propionibacterium acnes is TRUE?
fields.option_b: It is commonly associated with early-onset endophthalmitis
fields.option_c: It is an anaerobic Gram-positive bacillus associated with chronic postoperative endophthalmitis
fields.option_e: It is part of the respiratory flora
fields.explanation: Propionibacterium acnes (now Cutibacterium acnes) is an anaerobic, Gram-positive bacillus causing chronic low-grade postoperative endophthalmitis with delayed onset.

fields.text: A 19-year-old woman woke up 2 days ago with a red, photophobic left eye having accidentally slept in her contact lenses. Since then, the pain has increased. On examination, there is a 0.6 mm anterior stromal infiltrate with overlying epithelial defect. What is the most likely diagnosis?
fields.explanation: Pseudomonas aeruginosa is the most common cause of microbial keratitis in contact lens wearers. It produces rapid stromal necrosis and corneal perforation if untreated.

fields.text: Which feature best describes keratitis caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa?
fields.option_a: Slowly progressive with dry, raised ulcer edges
fields.option_b: Rapidly progressive with stromal melting and greenish exudate
fields.option_c: Peripheral ring infiltrate with satellite lesions
fields.option_d: Pain out of proportion to clinical findings
fields.explanation: Pseudomonas keratitis progresses rapidly, with stromal necrosis, mucopurulent discharge, and a characteristic greenish hue due to pyocyanin pigment.

fields.text: Which organism is a Gram-negative diplococcus capable of penetrating an intact corneal epithelium and causing hyperacute purulent conjunctivitis?
fields.explanation: Neisseria gonorrhoeae can invade intact corneal epithelium and cause severe purulent conjunctivitis and keratitis. It is a Gram-negative diplococcus.

fields.text: Moraxella keratitis is most commonly associated with which of the following risk factors?
fields.option_b: Chronic alcoholism and ocular surface disease
fields.explanation: Moraxella species cause keratitis in compromised corneas, especially in patients with alcoholism, diabetes, or chronic ocular surface disease.

fields.text: Which of the following ocular infections is most likely to show a 'collar-button' or hourglass-shaped ulcer on slit-lamp examination?
fields.explanation: Moraxella keratitis often produces a central or inferior ulcer with a 'collar-button' shape — an hourglass appearance due to stromal necrosis.

fields.text: A 3-year-old child presents with purulent discharge, preseptal swelling, and high fever. The likely organism is a small Gram-negative coccobacillus that requires both X and V factors for growth. What is the organism?
fields.explanation: Haemophilus influenzae is a small Gram-negative coccobacillus requiring both X (hemin) and V (NAD) factors for growth. It can cause preseptal cellulitis in children.

fields.text: Which of the following bacteria is most likely to cause chronic follicular conjunctivitis associated with lacrimal sac infection?
fields.explanation: Moraxella lacunata causes angular blepharoconjunctivitis and may be associated with chronic dacryocystitis or canalicular infection.

fields.text: Which virulence factor of Pseudomonas aeruginosa contributes to its tissue-destructive ability in keratitis?
fields.explanation: Exotoxin A produced by Pseudomonas aeruginosa inhibits protein synthesis by ADP-ribosylation of EF-2, leading to tissue necrosis and corneal melting.

fields.text: Which feature helps distinguish Pseudomonas keratitis from Acanthamoeba keratitis?
fields.option_e: Epithelial defect with underlying stromal infiltrate
fields.explanation: Pseudomonas keratitis shows rapid stromal destruction and mucopurulent discharge, while Acanthamoeba keratitis typically shows severe pain out of proportion to signs.

fields.text: Which antibiotic provides optimal coverage against Pseudomonas aeruginosa in severe bacterial keratitis?
fields.explanation: Ciprofloxacin, a fluoroquinolone, provides excellent coverage against Gram-negative organisms including Pseudomonas and is a first-line topical therapy in bacterial keratitis.

fields.text: A 25-year-old patient is known to have C3 deficiency. This is likely to predispose to which form of infection?
fields.explanation: C3 deficiency predisposes to life-threatening infections by encapsulated organisms such as Streptococcus pneumoniae (Pneumococcus).

fields.text: Deficiency of terminal complement components (C5–C9) is associated with recurrent infections by which organism?
fields.explanation: Terminal complement component deficiencies impair formation of the membrane attack complex (MAC), predisposing to recurrent Neisseria infections.

fields.text: A 10-year-old boy presents with recurrent pyogenic infections and albinism. Which of the following syndromes is most likely?
fields.explanation: Chediak–Higashi syndrome is a lysosomal trafficking defect leading to albinism and recurrent pyogenic infections due to defective phagolysosome fusion.

fields.text: Patients with chronic granulomatous disease (CGD) are predisposed to infections with which type of organisms?
fields.explanation: CGD results from a defect in NADPH oxidase, impairing oxidative killing of catalase-positive organisms like Staphylococcus aureus and Serratia marcescens.

fields.text: An infant presents with recurrent bacterial infections, absent lymphoid tissue, and low immunoglobulin levels. What is the most likely diagnosis?
fields.explanation: Bruton agammaglobulinemia is an X-linked disorder causing failure of B-cell maturation, leading to absent immunoglobulins and recurrent bacterial infections.

fields.text: Which immune deficiency is most associated with recurrent mucocutaneous Candida infections?
fields.explanation: Recurrent Candida infections suggest a T-cell defect, as cell-mediated immunity plays a key role in defense against fungal pathogens.

fields.text: A 3-year-old boy presents with recurrent infections, eczema, and thrombocytopenia. What is the most likely diagnosis?
